beats.title: Introduction to the Padma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter introduces the Padma Purana, discussing its significance as a Purana, its relation to the Vedas, and the nature of the divine. It explains that Puranas are expansions of the Vedas, and that the Padma Purana is considered the heart of the Puranic form of Lord Vishnu. The chapter also describes the roles of Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva as aspects of the same supreme being, and emphasizes the importance of listening to Puranas for spiritual knowledge.

beats.title: Introduction to the Padma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter is a preface or introduction to the Padma Purana, summarizing its contents and significance. It describes the Purana's coverage of various topics including the unity of Vishnu in multiple forms, the glories of Rama and Krishna, the importance of Vaishnava practices, and a wide range of religious duties, rituals, and teachings. The text emphasizes the Purana's comprehensive nature and its value for all Hindus.

beats.title: Padma Purana Part 3: Diverse Narratives and Teachings
beats.summary: This chapter of the Padma Purana (part 3) covers a wide range of topics including vratas (vows), tirtha (pilgrimage) mahatmya, stories of various deities, sages, kings, and demons, as well as teachings on dharma, charity, and devotion. Key narratives include Vamana avatar, Markandeya's longevity, Rama's pilgrimage, the birth of Ganesha and Kartikeya, the story of King Vena, and the importance of sat-sanga and pinda-dana.

beats.title: Complete (part 4)
beats.summary: This chapter of the Padma Purana covers a wide range of topics including the glories of Prayaga, devotion and name-chanting, duties of various ashramas (brahmacharya, grihastha, vanaprastha, sannyasa), the story of Rama's Ashvamedha sacrifice, the pastimes of Krishna in Vrindavan and Dvaraka, the importance of Vaishakha month, and various teachings on dharma and bhakti.

beats.title: Complete (part 5)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana (Part 5) is a table of contents listing various topics covered in the text, including rituals (vratas), holy places (tirthas), glories (mahatmya) of deities, rivers, and sacred days, as well as narratives of avatars like Rama and Krishna. It also mentions the Bhagavata Purana's chapters and their significance.

beats.title: Introduction to the Padma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter introduces the Padma Purana, beginning with the sage Ugrashravas (Suta) being instructed by his father Romaharshana to go to the sages' ashram and teach them dharma. Suta then narrates how he learned the Puranas from Vyasa. The sages at a sacrifice in Prayaga ask Suta to recite the Purana. Suta describes the origin of the Purana from Vishnu to Brahma, then to Marichi, and its divisions. He also recounts the story of King Prithu granting boons to Suta and Magadha. The chapter ends with a description of the cosmic egg and the emergence of Brahma.

beats.title: Introduction to Padma Purana and Creation Discourse
beats.summary: This chapter introduces the Padma Purana, describing its origin from Brahma to Pulastya, who then narrated it to Bhishma at Gangadwara. It emphasizes the importance of Puranas for understanding the Vedas. The narrative then shifts to Bhishma's meeting with Pulastya, where Bhishma asks about the creation of the universe. Pulastya begins explaining the creation process, starting with the emergence of Mahat, Ahankara, and the five elements.

beats.title: Cosmology and Creation in the Padma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the creation of the universe, the nature of the elements, the measurement of time (yugas, manvantaras, and Brahma's lifespan), and the roles of the Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva) in creation, preservation, and destruction. It also recounts the Varaha avatar rescuing the earth from the cosmic ocean.

beats.title: The Boar Incarnation and Creation of the World
beats.summary: This chapter describes the measurement of time in terms of manvantaras and yugas, the story of the boar incarnation (Varaha) lifting the earth from the cosmic ocean, the creation of the world by Brahma, and the classification of nine types of creation (sargas).

beats.title: Creation of the World and Genealogies
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of the world by Brahma, including the origin of the four varnas (Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaishyas, Shudras), the creation of various beings (devas, asuras, pitris, humans, etc.), the emergence of Rudra from Brahma's anger, the birth of the nine mind-born sons (Brahmarshis), the lineage of Svayambhuva Manu and his descendants, and the marriages of Daksha's daughters to various sages and deities. It also lists grains used in sacrifices and describes the rewards for righteous living and punishments for sin.

beats.title: Lineage of Dharma and Adharma, and the Churning of the Ocean
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineage of Dharma and Adharma, the birth of Lakshmi from Khyaati (wife of Bhrigu), and the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthan) where Lakshmi appears from the ocean and is given to Vishnu. It also covers the appearance of other beings like Surabhi, Varuni, Parijata, Apsaras, Chandra, Halahala poison, Dhanvantari, Uchchaihshravas, and Airavata.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and the Yajna of Daksha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the churning of the ocean, Vishnu's Mohini avatar distributing amrita to the devas, the defeat of the asuras, and then transitions to the story of Sati's self-immolation and Daksha's yajna. It includes the arrival of various deities and sages at the yajna, Sati's questioning of her father Daksha about Shiva's absence, and Daksha's explanation of why he did not invite Shiva.

beats.title: Sati's Self-Immolation and Rebirth; Genealogy of Beings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of Sati's anger at her father Daksha's insult to Shiva, her self-immolation, and later rebirth as the daughter of Himavan. It also includes a detailed account of the creation of various beings by Daksha and Kashyapa, listing many deities, sages, and other entities.

beats.title: Genealogies of the Gods, Demons, and Other Beings
beats.summary: This chapter describes the lineages of various beings born from the sage Kashyapa and his wives, including Aditi, Diti, Danu, Vinata, Kadru, and others. It lists the twelve Adityas, the sons of Diti (Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha), the descendants of Danu, the sons of Vinata (Garuda and Aruna), the Nagas from Kadru, and the Maruts from Diti. It also recounts Diti's penance to obtain a son to kill Indra, and the advice of sages regarding the Purnima vrata.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts and the Appointments of Lords
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Diti's pregnancy and Indra's destruction of the fetus, leading to the birth of the Maruts. It also describes the appointment of various deities as lords over different domains by Brahma, and lists the Manvantaras from Svayambhuva to Tamasa.

beats.title: Manvantaras and the Story of Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the succession of Manvantaras (ages of Manu), including the current Vaivasvata Manvantara and future ones. It also narrates the story of King Vena, his wickedness, his death, and the emergence of King Prithu from his body. Prithu is an avatar of Vishnu. The chapter details how Prithu and various beings milked the earth (as a cow) to obtain different substances.

beats.title: The Lineages of Surya and Chandra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the lineage of Surya (the Sun god) and Chandra (the Moon god), including the birth of Vivasvan, his wives, and their children. It narrates the story of Ila, who was transformed into a woman and later married Budha, giving birth to Pururavas, the founder of the Chandravamsha. It also details the Suryavamsha through Ikshvaku and other descendants.

beats.title: Solar Dynasty and Pitri Loka
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the solar dynasty (Suryavansha) lineage from Manu onwards, including kings such as Mandhata, Sagara, Bhagiratha, and Rama. It also details the seven classes of Pitris (ancestors) and their abodes, along with instructions for performing shraddha rituals.

beats.title: The Glory of Shraddha and the Pitris
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the nature of the Pitris (ancestors), their abode, and the proper performance of Shraddha rituals. It details the types of Pitris, the importance of offerings, the qualifications of Brahmins to be invited, and the procedures for Shraddha, including the use of specific materials and mantras.

beats.title: Śrāddha Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana provides detailed instructions on performing Śrāddha rituals, especially the Parvaṇa Śrāddha on Amāvāsyā. It describes the procedure for offering piṇḍas (rice balls) to ancestors, including the preparation of the vedi, placement of kuśa grass, invocation of pitṛs, offerings of water, incense, and food, and the disposal of piṇḍas. It also lists auspicious times for Śrāddha, such as specific tithis, nakṣatras, and saṅkrāntis, and emphasizes the importance of proper conduct, such as avoiding anger and maintaining purity. The chapter mentions deities like Viśvedevas and pitṛs, and references practices like Balivaishvadeva.

beats.title: Shraddha Ritual Procedure
beats.summary: This chapter describes the procedure for performing a Shraddha ritual, including the invocation of Vishvedevas and Pitris, offering of arghya, preparation of arghya vessels, placement of offerings, recitation of mantras, and the final blessings. It also mentions the types of food and texts that please the Pitris.

beats.title: Shraddha Vidhi and Rituals
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various types of Shraddha rituals, including Naimittika (occasional), Kamya (desire-fulfilling), Abhyudayika (prosperity), and Ekoddishta (for a single deceased). It details procedures for offerings, the role of Brahmins, rules for mourning periods (asoucha) based on varna, and the process of Sapindikarana (merging the deceased with ancestors). The chapter also discusses the importance of proper ritual conduct and the consequences of neglect.

beats.title: The Glory of Shraddha and Sacred Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance of performing Shraddha rituals for ancestors, listing numerous sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) where Shraddha, donations, and other rites yield immense and imperishable fruits. It explains how the offerings reach the ancestors in various forms depending on their birth, and emphasizes the role of tirthas like Pushkara, Gaya, and Prayaga in liberating the departed.

beats.title: The Glory of Gaya and the Origin of Chandra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the significance of performing shraddha and pinda-dana at Gaya, especially at Dharmaprishtha, Brahma-sara, and Gaya-shirsha-vata. It describes the story of a sage who watered mango trees and satisfied his ancestors simultaneously. The chapter also covers the origin of Chandra (the moon) from Atri's tears, his marriage to Daksha's daughters, his performance of a Rajasuya yajna with Vishnu's help, and the lineage of Pururava and his descendants. Additionally, it explains the divisions of the day (muhurtas) and the auspicious time for shraddha called kutapa.

beats.title: Yadu Dynasty and Kartavirya Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the lineage of Yadu, including the birth of Yadu and Turvasu from Devayani, and Druhyu, Anu, and Puru from Sharmishtha. It details the Yadava dynasty, focusing on King Kartavirya Arjuna (Sahasrabahu), his thousand arms, boons, and his encounter with Ravana. It also covers the lineage of Kroshtu, leading to the Vrishni dynasty and the future avatar of Krishna.

beats.title: Complete (part 26)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the genealogies of the Yadu dynasty, including the lineages of Satvata, Bhajamana, Devavridha, and others, culminating in the birth of Krishna. It lists many kings, their descendants, and marriages, with a focus on the Vrishni and Bhoja clans. The chapter also mentions the birth of the Pandavas and concludes with the appearance of Lord Krishna.

beats.title: The Incarnation of Krishna and the Glory of Pushkara Tirtha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the divine form of Lord Vishnu, his incarnation as Krishna, the lineage of Yadus, the birth of Krishna's sons and wives, and the glory of Pushkara Tirtha. It also discusses the three types of bhakti (devotion) and the rules for residing at Pushkara.

beats.title: Glory of Pushkara Tirtha and Duties of Ashramas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the types of devotion (bhakti) to Brahma, the glory of the Pushkara Tirtha, the rules for living there, and the duties of the four ashramas (brahmacharya, grihastha, vanaprastha, sannyasa). It also describes the benefits of dying in Pushkara and the importance of guru seva.

beats.title: Duties of Ashramas and Glorification of Renunciation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the duties and rules for householders (grihastha), the importance of honoring guests and performing rituals, the three types of livelihood for brahmins (kumbhadhanya, unchhavritti, kapoti), the transition to the vanaprastha (forest-dweller) stage, and the superiority of the sannyasa (renunciation) ashrama. It also lists various sages who practiced severe austerities.

beats.title: Brahma's Yajna at Pushkara and the Characteristics of a Sannyasi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the duties and characteristics of a true renunciant (sannyasi), emphasizing non-violence, equanimity, and devotion to Vishnu. It also narrates the story of Brahma's yajna at Pushkara, attended by various deities, sages, and beings, including Vishnu.

beats.title: Brahma's Sacrifice at Pushkara
beats.summary: This chapter describes Brahma's great sacrifice at Pushkara, attended by gods, sages, and demons. Vishnu guards the sacrifice. The demons boast of their power and express hostility towards the gods. Vishnu advises patience until the sacrifice ends, then war. Brahma pacifies the demons, who agree to behave. The sages perform rituals, and the lake Pushkara is named after their reflection (mukha-darśana). The chapter ends with the sages settling by the Sarasvati river.

beats.title: The Origin of Saraswati River and the Pushkara Tirtha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of the Saraswati river and its connection to the Pushkara Tirtha. It narrates how Brahma performed a grand sacrifice at Pushkara, where Saraswati appeared as a river to serve the sages. Later, at Vishnu's request, Saraswati carries the Bhadravanala (submarine fire) to the western ocean, with Brahma's permission. The chapter also mentions the meeting of Saraswati and Ganga, and the significance of the Plaksha tree (form of Vishnu).

beats.title: The Glory of Pushkara and the Naming of Saraswati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the sacredness of the Pushkara region, especially the Saraswati river. It narrates the origin of the Nanda name for Saraswati, the story of King Prabhanjana who shot a deer, and the subsequent appearance of Saraswati. The chapter extols the virtues of bathing, donating, and performing rituals at Pushkara, particularly at the three Pushkaras (Jyestha, Madhyama, and Kanistha).

beats.title: The Curse of the Doe and the Redemption through Nanda
beats.summary: A king accidentally kills a nursing doe and is cursed to become a tiger for a hundred years. The curse will end when he converses with a cow named Nanda. After a century, Nanda appears, and the tiger spares her after she promises to return after nursing her calf. The tiger trusts her oath.

beats.title: Nanda's Sacrifice
beats.summary: Nanda, a cow, instructs her calf on the importance of caution and distrust, then goes to offer herself to a tiger to keep her promise. She bids farewell to her son and friends, entrusting the calf to their care.

beats.title: The Story of Nanda the Cow and the Tiger
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Nanda, a cow who is a devotee of truth. She is confronted by a tiger who demands to eat her calf. Nanda refuses to lie to save herself, citing the supreme importance of truth. The tiger, impressed by her steadfastness, spares her and her calf, and they become siblings. The tiger then seeks guidance on atonement for his sins, and Nanda teaches him about the virtue of non-violence and the supremacy of truth.

beats.title: The Glory of Nanda Tirtha and Pushkara
beats.summary: The chapter describes the greatness of the Nanda tirtha and the Sarasvati river, the story of King Prabhanjana and a deer-turned-tiger, the importance of non-violence, and the glory of the Pushkara tirtha. It includes teachings on truthfulness, charity, and the benefits of bathing in sacred waters.

beats.title: The Glory of Pushkara and the Story of Dadhichi's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of Pushkara Tirtha, including the benefits of bathing, donating, and performing rituals there. It also narrates the story of how the sage Dadhichi sacrificed his body to provide his bones for the creation of the Vajra weapon, which Indra used to kill the demon Vritrasura.

beats.title: The Demon Menace and Agastya's Intervention
beats.summary: Demons led by Kārukeya hide in the ocean and terrorize sages, killing many. The gods pray to Vishnu, who reveals the demons' refuge and advises them to seek sage Agastya's help. Agastya is praised for his past deeds, including subduing the Vindhya mountain and drinking the ocean. The chapter ends with Agastya preparing to drink the ocean to expose the demons.

beats.title: Agastya Drinks the Ocean and the Sages' Discourse on Gifts
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of Sage Agastya drinking the ocean to help the gods defeat the demons, and later the gods seeking Brahma's advice to refill the ocean. It also includes a discourse on the dangers of accepting gifts (pratigraha) by sages, with examples from the Saptarshis and King Bhagiratha's future role in bringing the Ganga to fill the ocean.

beats.title: Discourses on Renunciation and the Glory of Food
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the evils of greed and attachment to wealth, the importance of contentment, the suffering caused by hunger, and the supreme value of food and food donation. It includes dialogues among sages such as Karayapji, Bharadvaja, Gautama, Vishvamitra, Jamadagni, and Asunthati, who emphasize renunciation, self-control, and the virtue of giving food.

beats.title: The Discourse on Self-Control and the Theft of Lotus Stalks
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of self-control (dama) and forgiveness (kshama) as the essence of dharma. It emphasizes that mere study of scriptures without control of the senses does not lead to liberation. The chapter also narrates a story of sages who, after performing rituals at a lake, find their lotus stalks stolen, leading to a discussion on theft and its consequences. The sage Pulastya instructs on the supreme dharma of treating others' wives as mothers, others' wealth as dirt, and all beings as one's own self.

beats.title: Vows and Merits at Pushkara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various vows (vrata) and their associated rituals, including the Mahapatakanashana vrata, Gauri vrata, Rudra vrata, Somya vrata, Saubhagya vrata, Sarasvata vrata, Sama vrata, Vira vrata, Ananda vrata, Dipa vrata, and Rudra vrata. It also includes a dialogue between sages and Indra about the merits of visiting Pushkara and performing donations.

beats.title: Description of Various Vows and Their Rewards
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various vows (vratas) and their associated rituals, including fasting, donations of gold, cows, and other items, and the resulting rewards such as attaining specific heavenly realms or liberation. It also discusses the importance of bathing and recitation of mantras.

beats.title: The Story of King Dharmamūrti and the Power of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter describes the sacred names and glories of the river Ganga (Jahnavi), the ritual of bathing and offering tarpaṇa (libations) to various beings including deities, sages, pitṛs, and others, and the story of King Dharmamūrti and his queen Bhānumatī. It also includes a narrative about a goldsmith and a maiden named Līlāvatī, illustrating the power of devotion and righteous deeds.

beats.title: The Bhimadvadashi Vrata and the Donation of Grain Mountains
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the benefits of donating grain mountains and the ritual of Bhimadvadashi vrata, including detailed procedures for worship of Vishnu, Lakshmi, and Garuda, as well as the construction of a mandapa and the performance of a night-long water stream ritual.

beats.title: Glory of Kalyani Tithi and Bhima Dvadashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of the Kalyani Tithi (auspicious day) and the Bhima Dvadashi vrata, including its origin from Bhimasena's observance. It details the Aditya-Shayana vrata, Rohini-Chandra vrata, tank installation, tree planting, and the procedure for the Aditya-Shayana vrata with specific rituals for each nakshatra. The chapter also includes instructions for donating a cow and the importance of keeping the vrata secret from unworthy persons.

beats.title: The Chandrashayana Vrata and Its Glories
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Adityadana (or Chandrashayana) vrata, its benefits, and the procedure for its observance, including worship of Lord Narayana in the form of Chandra (moon) and Rohini. It also details the construction of altars and offerings for the vrata, and mentions the benefits of hearing or reciting this vrata's glory.

beats.title: Ritual for Constructing a Sacred Water Reservoir
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the detailed procedure for constructing a sacred water reservoir (tadaga) and its associated rituals, including the setup of a yupa (sacrificial post), selection of priests, offerings, and the establishment of deities. It also explains the varying fruits of such constructions based on the duration of water retention.

beats.title: Tree Consecration and Saubhagya-sayana Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the ritual for planting and consecrating trees, including the benefits of planting various species. It also narrates the origin of the 'Saubhagya-sayana' vrata and the worship of Goddess Parvati and Lord Shiva on the third day of the bright fortnight of Chaitra.

beats.title: Saubhagya-ashthaka Vrata and Vamana Avatar
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Saubhagya-ashthaka vrata (a ritual for marital bliss) involving offerings to Shiva and Parvati, and narrates the story of Vamana avatar where Vishnu as a dwarf Brahmin asks for three paces of land from the demon king Baskali, ultimately defeating him.

beats.title: Vamana's Visit to Baskali
beats.summary: The chapter describes the appearance of Lord Vamana, the dwarf incarnation of Vishnu, and his journey to the city of Baskali, a virtuous demon king. Indra accompanies Vamana, and Baskali receives them with great honor. Indra requests Baskali to grant Vamana three paces of land, as Vamana had asked of Indra, but Indra is unable to fulfill the request himself. Baskali agrees to give the land.

beats.title: The Vamana Avatar and King Bali
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of King Bali (Bashkali) and the Vamana avatar of Vishnu. Bali, having conquered the three worlds, is approached by Vamana (a dwarf brahmin) who asks for three paces of land. Despite warnings from his guru Shukracharya that Vamana is Vishnu in disguise, Bali insists on fulfilling his promise. Vamana then expands to cosmic size, covering the earth and heavens in two steps, and places the third step on Bali's head, sending him to the netherworld. Bali is granted a boon to be killed by Vishnu in a future kalpa and attain liberation.

beats.title: Vamana Avatar and the Liberation of Five Pretas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Vamana avatar of Vishnu, the story of the demon Baskala being bound, and the liberation of five pretas (ghosts) through the grace of a Brahmin. It also includes teachings on the consequences of sin and the importance of charity and purity.

beats.title: Causes of Preta and Glory of Pushkara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the causes of becoming a pret (ghost) and the means to avoid that state. It also describes the glory of Pushkara tirtha and the Sarasvati river, especially during Kartika Purnima.

beats.title: Glories of Prācī Sarasvatī and Mārkaṇḍeya's Long Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of the Prācī Sarasvatī tirtha, including the benefits of donations, fasting, and rituals performed there. It narrates the meeting of Gaṅgā and Sarasvatī, the praise of Sarasvatī by the gods, and the story of Mārkaṇḍeya's long life. It also recounts Lord Rāmacandra's visit to Puṣkara to perform śrāddha for his father Daśaratha, and the establishment of the Āditya tīrtha.

beats.title: Markandeya's Boon and Rama's Shraddha at Pushkara
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Markandeya's boon of long life from Brahma, his father's joy, and later Rama's pilgrimage to Pushkara where he performs shraddha for his father Dasharatha. It includes descriptions of sacred sites and the importance of shraddha rituals.

beats.title: Sita's Vision and Rama's Hymn to Shiva
beats.summary: Sita describes to Rama a wondrous event where, upon Rama's utterance of names, King Sagar and two similar figures appeared, and she saw her ancestors in the bodies of Brahmins. Rama then performs a shraddha ritual. Later, Rama and Lakshmana travel to Pushkara, visit a shrine of Shiva, and Rama offers a hymn of praise to Shiva, recounting his deeds.

beats.title: Complete (part 59)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the worship of Lord Shiva (Shankara) as the refuge-giver, his various forms and deeds, including cutting off Brahma's fifth head, rescuing a sage from Yama's messengers, and his association with Parvati and Nandi. It also narrates the story of Lord Rama's encounter with Shiva at the Narmada river, and details the organization of Brahma's yajna (sacrifice) with various rishis as priests, the distribution of dakshina, and the granting of boons to deities.

beats.title: Brahma's Boons and Vishnu's Hymn
beats.summary: Brahma grants boons to Vishnu and Shiva, declaring that wherever their images or lingas are present, that place becomes a holy tirtha. He promises freedom from disease, fear, and sorrow for those who worship them. Vishnu then praises Brahma with a hymn, describing his cosmic form and supremacy. Brahma responds by listing his various names and forms at different tirthas.

beats.title: The Glory of Pushkara Tirtha and Rama's Deeds
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana lists 108 sacred places (tīrthas) where Lord Shiva resides, describing the benefits of visiting them, especially Pushkara. It also narrates the story of Lord Rama's encounter with sages, including Agastya, and his slaying of Shambuka and revival of a dead Brahmin boy.

beats.title: शम्बूक वध और ब्राह्मण बालक का पुनरुत्थान
beats.summary: श्रीराम द्वारा शम्बूक वध और मृत ब्राह्मण बालक को जीवनदान।

beats.title: The Divine Ornament and the Story of King Mucukunda
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes a conversation between Lord Rama and Sage Agastya. Agastya offers Rama a divine ornament, but Rama hesitates to accept it, citing the sin of accepting gifts from a Brahmin. Agastya then narrates the story of King Mucukunda, who after performing severe penance, was cursed to eat his own body daily until Agastya would liberate him. The chapter also explains the origin of kings from the combined tejas of the Lokapalas (Indra, Varuna, Kubera, Yama).

beats.title: The Curse of King Danda and the Liberation of King Shveta
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Danda, who commits a heinous crime by forcibly abducting and violating Araja, the daughter of sage Shukracharya. As a result, Shukracharya curses Danda and his kingdom to be destroyed by a rain of dust. The chapter also includes a sub-story about King Shveta who offers a divine ornament to sage Agastya and attains liberation.

beats.title: Rama's Journey to Lanka and Establishment of Vamana
beats.summary: This chapter describes Shri Rama's journey to Lanka with Bharata and Sugriva, meeting Vibhishana, and the establishment of the Vamana deity in Kanyakubja. It includes Rama's departure from Agastya's ashram, his return to Ayodhya, and subsequent travels to various holy places.

beats.title: Post-War Events in Lanka and Installation of Rameshvara
beats.summary: This chapter describes events after the construction of the bridge to Lanka, the battle with Ravana, and the subsequent events including the meeting with Vibhishana, the arrival of various beings, the establishment of the Vamana deity, and the installation of the Rameshvara linga.

beats.title: Rudra's Instructions to Rama and Encounter with Brahma
beats.summary: Lord Rudra appears before Rama and praises him as Narayana. Rudra instructs Rama to establish a shrine of Vamana on the Ganges bank and promises that those who visit the site will be freed from sins. Rama then proceeds in his aerial chariot but is stopped at Pushkara where Brahma is seated with deities. Rama descends and offers obeisance to Brahma.

beats.title: Establishment of Vamana Deity and Discourse on Yugas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the establishment of Lord Vamana's deity on the banks of the Ganga by Lord Rama, the dialogue between Brahma and Rama, the return to Ayodhya, and the meeting with Shatrughna. It also includes a discourse on the four yugas (Satya, Treta, Dvapara, Kali) and the nature of Lord Narayana.

beats.title: The Great Dissolution and Markandeya's Vision
beats.summary: This chapter describes the great dissolution (mahapralaya) where Lord Narayana as the sun absorbs the ocean, then as wind agitates the world, and the elements dissolve into each other. Brahma merges into Hrishikesha. After the dissolution, Lord Vishnu rests on the cosmic waters. The sage Markandeya enters Vishnu's belly and travels within, seeing the entire earth. After a hundred years, he exits and sees a child on a banyan leaf, who reveals himself as Narayana, the supreme being, and explains his identity as all gods, elements, and the ultimate reality. Markandeya then re-enters Vishnu's belly to hear dharma from the swan (hamsa) form of the Lord.

beats.title: Creation and the Slaying of Madhu and Kaitabha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of the lotus from Vishnu's navel, the emergence of Brahma, the slaying of the demons Madhu and Kaitabha, and the subsequent creation of various beings including the Rudras, Vasus, Adityas, and others. It also includes the story of Tarakasura's birth and the consolation of the gods by Brahma.

beats.title: The Birth and Boon of Tarakasura
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the demon king Vajranga, his wife Varangi, and their son Tarakasura. Vajranga captures Indra but releases him at Brahma's request. He performs severe penance and receives a boon. His wife Varangi is harassed by Indra, leading Vajranga to seek a powerful son. Brahma grants him Tarakasura, who becomes a mighty demon king. Tarakasura performs penance and obtains a boon that his death can only occur from a seven-day-old child. He then prepares for war against the gods.

beats.title: The Defeat of Tārakāsura and the Marriage of Śiva and Pārvatī
beats.summary: Bṛhaspati advises Indra on the fourfold policy (sāma, bheda, dāna, daṇḍa) against the demon Tārakāsura. The gods prepare for war but are defeated. They seek refuge in Brahmā, who reveals that Tārakāsura can only be killed by a son of Śiva born from Umā (Pārvatī). Indra then summons Nārada to arrange the marriage of Śiva and Pārvatī.

beats.title: Narada's Prophecy about Parvati
beats.summary: Narada visits Himavan and describes the future of his daughter Uma (Parvati), explaining that her seemingly inauspicious bodily signs actually indicate her divine nature as the consort of Shiva and mother of the universe.

beats.title: Shiva Burns Kamadeva
beats.summary: Pulastya narrates the story of Shiva and Parvati's marriage. After hearing the words of the sage, Himalaya and his wife Menaka rejoice. Narada advises Himalaya to arrange Parvati's marriage with Shiva. Indra summons Kamadeva to disturb Shiva's meditation. Kamadeva, with Rati and Vasanta, attempts to arouse desire in Shiva, but Shiva opens his third eye and burns Kamadeva to ashes. Rati laments and prays to Shiva for her husband's return.

beats.title: Parvati's Penance and the Sages' Visit
beats.summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Kama's destruction by Shiva, Rati's lament and Shiva's boon that Kama will be reborn as Ananga. It then narrates Parvati's determination to win Shiva as her husband, her severe penance in the Himalayas, the visit of the seven sages sent by Indra to dissuade her, and her eloquent defense of Shiva's supreme nature, which leads the sages to report to Shiva.

beats.title: The Marriage of Shiva and Parvati and the Birth of Ganesha and Kartikeya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the marriage of Parvati and Shiva, including Parvati's birth, the burning of Madana, her penance, and the wedding. It also covers the birth of Ganesha and Kartikeya, and Kartikeya's slaying of Tarakasura.

beats.title: Birth of Kartikeya and Slaying of Tarakasura
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of Kartikeya (Skanda) from Parvati and Shiva, his adoption by the six Krittikas, his investiture as the commander of the divine army, and his battle with the demon Tarakasura, whom he ultimately kills.

beats.title: The Slaying of Tarakasura and the Glory of Brahmins
beats.summary: The chapter describes the battle between Kartikeya and Tarakasura, culminating in Tarakasura's death by Kartikeya's spear. It then transitions to a discourse on the importance of Brahmins, their qualities, and the significance of the Gayatri mantra, as narrated by Brahma to Narada.

beats.title: The Glory of Gayatri and the Practice of Nyasa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Gayatri mantra, its deities, nyasa (placement) on the body, and the benefits of japa (chanting) with pranayama. It also discusses the qualities of a pure devotee of Narayana and the importance of brahmins who are devoted to japa.

beats.title: The Glory of Gayatri and the Characteristics of Brahmins
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of the Gayatri mantra, its proper recitation with bodily gestures, and the consequences of not knowing it. It describes the ten types of baths, the characteristics of an inferior Brahmin, and the fate of fallen Brahmins. The chapter also emphasizes the supremacy of Gayatri over the four Vedas and the benefits of chanting it. Finally, it addresses the redemption of a sinful Brahmin through self-control.

beats.title: The Fallen Brahmin and Garuda's Encounter with Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter narrates two stories: a Brahmin who fell from grace due to association with a Chandali but was purified by his Brahminical tejas, and the story of Garuda's birth, his hunger, his meeting with his father Kashyapa, and his encounter with Vishnu who becomes his eternal mount.

beats.title: Complete (part 82)
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Garuda's birth, his receiving boons from Vishnu, his rescue of his mother Vinata from slavery by stealing amrita from the gods, and his encounter with Indra. It also includes teachings on the glory of Vishnu devotees and the importance of good conduct.

beats.title: Discourse on Brahminical Duties and Consequences
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of good conduct (sadachara), the severe consequences of harming Brahmins, the duties and livelihoods of Brahmins, the sin of Brahminicide (brahmahatya), and the merits of cows and cow donation. It also describes the eight types of leprosy and their causes, and the proper conduct in righteous war (dharmayuddha).

beats.title: Duties of Kshatriyas and Brahmins, and the Glory of Cows
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the ethics of warfare for Kshatriyas, the duties of Brahmins during adversity, the importance of truthfulness, and the sacredness of cows. It describes the consequences of righteous and unrighteous combat, the permissibility of trade and agriculture for Brahmins, and the proper care of cows, emphasizing their divine nature and the sin of harming them.

beats.title: Glory of the Cow and Duties of a Brahmin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the glory and sacredness of cows (gau), describing them as the abode of various deities and the source of all auspiciousness. It details the benefits of cow worship, donation, and the recitation of divine names, as well as the duties of a Brahmin. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Narada and Brahma on the merits of donating cows of different colors and the consequences of harming them.

beats.title: The Importance of Bathing and Tarpana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance of daily bathing (snana) and the ritual of offering water to ancestors (tarpana). It details the purifying power of water at different times of the day, the types of sacred grasses (kusha) used in rituals, and the proper procedures for tarpana to please the ancestors and deities. The chapter emphasizes that bathing and tarpana, especially with til (sesame) and kusha, can absolve sins and lead to spiritual rewards such as reaching Vishnu's abode or Brahmaloka.

beats.title: Instructions on Daily Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses daily rituals and proper conduct (sadachara) for a householder, including purification of vessels, bathing, offering tarpana to ancestors, rules for eating, and respectful behavior towards deities, brahmins, gurus, and women. It emphasizes that following these practices leads to long life, wealth, heaven, and liberation.

beats.title: The Tale of Narottama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses rituals for purification (achamana), proper sleeping directions, the four types of food offerings, and the five great sacrifices (pancha mahayajnas) centered on devotion to parents, spouse, equality, non-hostility, and Vishnu. It includes a story of a Brahmin named Narottama who neglected his parents and suffered consequences, emphasizing the importance of honoring parents.

beats.title: The Brahmin's Quest for Dharma
beats.summary: A Brahmin, guided by a divine voice, seeks a mute Chandala who serves his parents. The Chandala directs him to a pativrata (devoted wife), who in turn sends him to a merchant named Tuladhara. Throughout, the Brahmin encounters Vishnu in disguise and learns about the qualities of a pativrata.

beats.title: The Virtue of Tuladhara and Adrohaka
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of a virtuous merchant named Tuladhara and a householder named Adrohaka. A brahmin, seeking dharma, is directed by Tuladhara to Adrohaka. Adrohaka, known for his truthfulness and equanimity, is asked by a prince to protect his beautiful wife Sundari. Adrohaka agrees but treats her with disrespect to safeguard her chastity. When the prince returns, Adrohaka, distressed by public censure, enters a fire but remains unharmed due to his virtue. The chapter extols truth, equanimity, and the protection of dharma.

beats.title: The Story of Adrohaka and the Brahmin's Vision
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of a prince named Adrohaka who, through his virtues and devotion, gains divine vision and is honored by gods. A brahmin, guided by a disguised Vishnu, seeks a Vaishnava and eventually receives a vision of Vishnu, who teaches the importance of honoring parents and the power of association with holy beings.

beats.title: The Virtues of Devotion to Parents and the Lord's Presence
beats.summary: The chapter discusses the virtues of devotion to parents, truthfulness, equanimity, non-enmity, and devotion to Vishnu. It describes how the Lord resides in the homes of those who possess these qualities, such as a mute Chandala, a faithful wife, Tuladhara, Adrohaka, and a Vaishnava. It also explains the importance of performing shraddha for parents, especially during eclipses and the Mahalaya period, and the benefits of dying in holy places like the Ganges.

beats.title: The Story of the Pativrata Brahmani Rauvya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the consequences of improper death rites, the importance of performing shraddha and other rituals for the deceased, and the story of a devoted wife (pativrata) named Rauvya who serves her leprous husband and tries to fulfill his desire for a courtesan. It also includes teachings on the merit of reciting this chapter and the glory of pativrata dharma.

beats.title: The Devoted Wife and the Sage's Curse
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a devoted wife (pativrata) who carries her leprous husband to a prostitute's house to fulfill his desire. On the way, they disturb the meditation of sage Mandavya, who curses the husband to die at sunrise. The wife then stops the sun from rising, causing cosmic distress. Brahma and other gods intervene, and the wife explains her priority is her husband's life. Ultimately, the husband dies but is reborn handsome, and the wife's devotion is praised. The chapter also discusses karmic causes of their past lives.

beats.title: Discourse on Women's Nature and Duties
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the nature of women, emphasizing the importance of chastity and the dangers of female infidelity. It includes a dialogue between Lord Vishnu and a Brahmin, and references the Uma-Narada dialogue as an example. The text prescribes severe punishments for sexual transgressions, extols the virtues of faithful wives (pativratas), and glorifies the chanting of Govinda's name for purification.

beats.title: The Story of Tuladhara and the Power of Truth
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the merits of giving a daughter in marriage (kanyadana), the importance of truthfulness, and the story of Tuladhara, a truthful merchant. It warns against accepting dowry, selling daughters, and giving daughters to unworthy men. The chapter also extols the power of truth, citing examples of King Yudhishthira and the sage Tuladhara, and describes the consequences of greed and falsehood.

beats.title: The Shudra and the Kshapanaka
beats.summary: A dialogue between a Shudra and a Kshapanaka (Jain ascetic) about the merits and demerits of wealth. The Shudra rejects wealth due to its dangers, advocating detachment. The Kshapanaka reveals himself as Vishnu, and the Shudra and his family attain heaven. The chapter also extols the virtues of building ponds, planting trees, and serving parents.

beats.title: The Merits of Digging Ponds and Planting Trees
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the merits of digging ponds, planting trees (especially the peepal tree), and performing worship. It includes a story of a wealthy man who built a pond and later a dispute over its ownership, resolved by a miraculous floating stone. The chapter emphasizes the spiritual benefits of such acts, including attaining heaven and liberation.

beats.title: Merits of Temple Construction and Rudraksha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the merits and demerits of cutting certain trees (especially peepul), the construction and maintenance of water-sheds (pausala), bridges, and temples, the importance of donating grazing land (gocharabhumi), the worship of Shiva and Vishnu, the significance of Rudraksha beads, and the consequences of misusing temple funds.

beats.title: The Glory of Rudraksha and Amla
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the origin and glory of rudraksha beads, the benefits of amla (Indian gooseberry), and the fate of sinners who become pretas (ghosts). It includes a dialogue between Shiva and Kartikeya, and a story of a chandala who attained liberation through contact with amla.

beats.title: The Liberation of a Pret through Amla
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the causes of becoming a pret (ghost) and the means to avoid that state. It emphasizes the importance of holy actions such as bathing in tirthas, worshipping Vishnu, observing fasts, reciting Vedas, wearing rudraksha, and especially consuming and using amla (Indian gooseberry) and tulsi (holy basil). A story is told of a pret who attained heaven by eating amla fruit. The chapter also describes the virtues of tulsi and its power to grant liberation.

beats.title: The Glorification of Tulsi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the virtues of the Tulsi (basil) plant, describing its origin from the churning of the ocean, its association with Vishnu, and the benefits of serving, planting, and worshipping it. It also praises the Ganges river and includes a Tulsi stotra (hymn) that grants liberation and forgiveness of sins.

beats.title: Glory of the Ganges
beats.summary: This chapter extols the supreme glory and purifying power of the Ganges River (Ganga). It describes how bathing in, drinking, or even touching Ganga's water liberates beings from sins, grants heaven or moksha, and uplifts ancestors. The chapter also provides a mantra for Ganga worship and emphasizes the merit of visiting, praising, and helping others to reach Ganga.

beats.title: Complete (part 104)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the origin and glory of the Ganges river, the importance of Ganesha worship, and the benefits of pilgrimage to sacred sites like Haridwar, Prayag, and Ganga-Sagar. It includes dialogues between sages and deities, emphasizing the purifying power of the Ganges and the necessity of worshipping Ganesha first in all rituals.

beats.title: Ganesha Worship and Characteristics of Humans
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the worship of Ganesha, including his twelve names, mantras, and the benefits of his worship. It also discusses the characteristics of demons, rakshasas, and deities born in human form, with a focus on distinguishing good and evil traits. The chapter includes a dialogue between Sanjaya and Vyasa about identifying asuras and devas among humans, and lists several divine incarnations from the Mahabharata.

beats.title: The Glory of Surya and the Pacification of His Tejas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of righteous conduct, worship of deities (especially Surya), and the consequences of sinful actions. It describes the origin and glory of Surya, his role as the supreme deity, and the benefits of his worship. The narrative includes a dialogue between sages and gods, highlighting Surya's power and the need for his pacification.

beats.title: Complete (part 107)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Sun god (Surya), his various names and forms, the significance of Sankranti (solar transitions), and the merits of observing Sunday fasts and performing rituals dedicated to Surya. It also narrates how Brahma instructed Vishvakarma to reduce the intensity of Surya's rays, leading to the creation of divine weapons.

beats.title: Complete (part 108)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the twelve names of the Sun god (Surya) and their benefits, a secret mantra (mūkamantra) for worship, and the story of King Bhadreśvara who was cured of leprosy by worshipping Surya. It emphasizes the power of Surya's names and mantra for removing sins, diseases, and granting desires.

beats.title: The Devotion of Shivasharma's Sons
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of a Brahmin named Shivasharma and his five sons who are devoted to their father. Through a series of tests, the sons prove their filial piety, with the son Vedasharma even offering his own head at his father's command. The chapter extols the virtue of devotion to one's father as the highest form of duty.

beats.title: The Devotion of Vishnurma and the Power of Filial Piety
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Vishnurma (or Vishnudarma), a devoted son who goes to Indra's realm to obtain amrita for his ailing father. Despite obstacles sent by Indra, including the celestial nymph Menaka, Vishnurma remains steadfast. Indra eventually relents, grants the amrita, and blesses him. Later, Vishnurma's father revives his mother from Yama's realm. The father offers boons to his sons; four choose to go to Vaikuntha, while the youngest, Somadharma, stays. Vishnu appears and transports the four sons to His abode, granting them divine forms.

beats.title: The Devotion of Somasharma and the Introduction of Sutrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of Somasharma, a devoted son who serves his parents despite their feigned leprosy and harsh treatment. His unwavering piety and truthfulness cause an empty pot to fill with nectar, leading to his parents' revelation and departure to Vishnu's abode. The chapter also introduces the story of Sutrata, a Brahmin born in the Kaurika lineage near the Narmada river, and includes a discourse on the tree of dharma and adharma.

beats.title: Discourse on Karmic Relationships and Dharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the nature of relationships, particularly between parents and children, as determined by karmic debts from past lives. It describes three types of sons: those who cause suffering (repaying debts), those who bring joy (repaying kindness), and indifferent ones. The dialogue between Somasharma and his wife Sumanā emphasizes detachment, the importance of virtuous deeds (puṇya), and the path to liberation through devotion and ethical living. The chapter also references the sages Dattātreya and Durvāsā as exemplars of dharma.

beats.title: Durvasa's Curse on Dharma and Discourse on Virtues
beats.summary: In this chapter, Dharma personified appears before the sage Durvasa accompanied by various virtues and deities. Durvasa, angered by what he perceives as Dharma's partiality, curses Dharma to be born as a king, a son of a maidservant, and a chandala. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Sumanā and Somashravas about the nature of brahmacharya, truth, and other virtues.

beats.title: Discourse on Dharma and Death
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the characteristics of dharma (righteousness) including qualities like contentment, forgiveness, purity, non-violence, and peace. It describes the signs of a righteous person's death, which is peaceful and attended by divine beings, versus the painful death of a sinner, who is tormented by Yama's messengers. The narrative includes a dialogue between Somarama and his wife Sumana, who explains these teachings, which she learned from her father, the sage Chyavana, and a Siddha who visited their ashram.

beats.title: The Fate of Sinners and the Story of Somadama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the fate of sinners after death, including being dragged by Yama's messengers to Yamaloka, where they are judged by Yama and Chitragupta and subjected to severe punishments for thousands of yugas. It then narrates the story of Somadama, who, due to his past life as a greedy and irreligious Shudra, suffers poverty and lack of sons. Sage Vasishtha explains his past life and advises him to worship Vishnu to obtain a virtuous son.

beats.title: The Devotion of Somasharma
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a Brahmin named Somasharma and his wife Sumana, who perform austerities and worship Lord Vishnu. It describes their encounter with a sage, observance of Ekadashi, and the eventual vision of Vishnu. The chapter emphasizes the importance of devotion, charity, and the power of Vishnu's grace.

beats.title: Somasharman's Penance and Boon
beats.summary: Sage Somasharman performs intense penance and recites a hymn to Lord Vishnu, who appears before him. Vishnu grants him a boon, and Somasharman receives a son named Suvrata, who later attains Vaikuntha with his parents.

beats.title: The Birth and Devotion of Suvrata
beats.summary: Somaśarmā, a brāhmaṇa, prays to Lord Hari for a son devoted to Viṣṇu. The Lord grants his wish, and his wife Sumanā gives birth to Suvrata, who from childhood is absorbed in meditation on Viṣṇu, seeing Him everywhere and singing His glories. The chapter describes Suvrata's divine perception and devotion.

beats.title: The Story of Suvrata and the Boon of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of Suvrata, a devout son of the brahmin Somasharma and his wife Sumana. Suvrata is deeply devoted to Lord Vishnu, constantly meditating on him even while eating, sleeping, or performing daily activities. He renounces worldly pleasures in youth and performs severe penance on the Vaidurya mountain. Pleased with his devotion, Lord Vishnu appears before him with Lakshmi. Suvrata prays for liberation for himself, his parents, and his wife. Vishnu grants the boon and takes them all to Vaikuntha. The chapter also mentions that the story of King Prithu's birth and deeds will be narrated later.

beats.title: The Birth and Deeds of King Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth and character of King Prithu. It begins with the wicked king Vena, who abandons dharma and declares himself the supreme deity. The sages, angered by his blasphemy, kill him by chanting mantras. From Vena's left thigh emerges a dark, ugly man named Nishada, progenitor of the Nishada tribes. From his right arm emerges the radiant Prince Prithu, an incarnation of Vishnu. Prithu is crowned king and brings a golden age: the earth yields crops without plowing, cows become wish-fulfilling, and all beings are happy. When the earth hides the seeds, Prithu chases her in anger. She takes the form of a cow and begs for mercy. Prithu spares her and milks her for the benefit of all creatures.

beats.title: The Milking of the Earth by King Prithu
beats.summary: King Prithu, angered by Earth's withholding of resources, threatens to kill her. Earth, in the form of a cow, pleads for mercy and suggests that Prithu level her surface so that milk (produce) can flow. Prithu uses his bow to uproot mountains and make Earth level. Then various beings milk Earth for different substances: sages for the Vedas, gods for soma, ancestors for offerings, serpents for poison, demons for illusion, etc. Earth becomes a wish-fulfilling cow. Prithu is praised as the establisher of dharma and the one who made Earth habitable.

beats.title: The Story of Sunitha and King Anga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of Sunitha, the daughter of Mrityu (Death), who mistreats a Gandharva prince in the forest. The Gandharva curses her that she will bear a wicked son. Later, King Anga, son of Atri, performs severe penance to please Lord Vishnu and obtain a son like Indra. Vishnu appears and grants his wish. The chapter also discusses the purifying power of holy rivers and the company of saints.

beats.title: Anga's Hymn and Boon; Sunitha's Penance
beats.summary: This chapter contains a hymn of praise to Vishnu (Narayana) by the sage Anga, followed by Vishnu granting him a boon for a son. It also narrates the story of Sunitha, who after being cursed by the Gandharva Sura, goes to the forest to perform penance, advised by her father to seek truth and good company.

beats.title: सुनीथा का वृत्तांत और वेन का जन्म
beats.summary: सुनीथा, जिसे सुरांक ने शाप दिया था, वन में दुःखी है। उसकी सखियाँ रम्भा आदि आती हैं और उसे चिंता त्यागने का उपदेश देती हैं। वे उसे एक मोहिनी विद्या देती हैं। सुनीथा उस विद्या से एक तपस्वी ब्राह्मण (अंग) को मोहित करती है और गंधर्व विवाह करती है, जिससे वेन का जन्म होता है।

beats.title: The Story of King Vena's Downfall
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Anga, who meets the beautiful maiden Sunitha in a forest and marries her. They have a son named Vena, who becomes a king. However, Vena is misled by a Jain ascetic disguised as a sage, who preaches a heretical doctrine rejecting Vedic rituals and promoting non-violence and worship of Arhat. Vena adopts this path, leading to his downfall.

beats.title: The Downfall and Redemption of King Vena
beats.summary: King Vena, influenced by a demonic figure, rejects Vedic dharma, tirthas, and charity, leading to chaos. Sages kill him, then churn his body to produce a low-born man and later King Prithu. Vishnu appears to Vena, explains his past, and teaches the importance of charity and tirthas.

beats.title: Discourse on Charity by Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of charity (dana) performed with devotion, especially on auspicious occasions. It describes various types of dana: nitya (daily), naimittika (occasional), kamya (desire-motivated), and abhyudayika (prosperity-oriented). It also lists sacred rivers, tirthas (pilgrimage sites), and qualities of worthy recipients of charity, while warning against giving to unworthy individuals. The chapter includes a brief mention of the story of Sati Sukala and emphasizes the role of faith (shraddha) in charitable acts.

beats.title: The Story of Sukala and Krikala
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the merits of various donations (dana), especially during auspicious occasions, and emphasizes the importance of charity with faith. It narrates the story of a virtuous wife named Sukala who extols the husband as the supreme tirtha (holy place) for a wife, and her husband Krikala's dilemma about taking her on a pilgrimage.

beats.title: Sukala's Devotion and the Story of Sudeva
beats.summary: Sukala, a devoted wife, is distressed when her husband Krikala leaves for a pilgrimage. She vows to fast and remain chaste until his return. Her friends try to console her, but she insists on the importance of serving one's husband. She then narrates the story of Queen Sudeva, wife of King Ikshvaku, who went hunting and encountered a boar.

beats.title: The Boar King's Last Stand
beats.summary: A boar king (शूकर) prepares to fight King Ikshvaku, who has come hunting. The boar's wife (शुकरी) pleads with him to flee, but he insists on fighting, citing dharma and the glory of battle. The boar's family and warriors engage in a fierce battle with Ikshvaku's hunters, resulting in heavy casualties. The boar refuses to retreat, emphasizing the virtue of facing death in battle.

beats.title: The Boar and the Sow
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a boar and a sow (shukara and shukari) in the context of King Ikshvaku's hunt. The sow, after her mate is killed, fights fiercely to protect her young. She is eventually wounded and speaks in Sanskrit, revealing her past life as a gandharva woman. The chapter also includes a sub-story about a gandharva named Sangavidyadhara who disturbed a sage's meditation with his music.

beats.title: The Curse of the Boar and the Tale of Sudharma
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a sage named Pulastya who is disturbed by a gandharva named Gitavidyadhara, who takes the form of a boar to mock him. Pulastya curses him to be born as a boar. Later, a female boar (the cursed gandharva's wife) recounts her past life as Sudharma, a Brahmin woman who was proud and disobedient to her husband, leading to her current birth. The chapter includes teachings on parenting and the consequences of one's actions.

beats.title: The Deception of Padmavati
beats.summary: King Ugrasena of Mathura marries Padmavati, daughter of King Satyaketu of Vidarbha. They live happily, but later Padmavati visits her parents. While bathing in a lake, she is seen by the demon Gobhila, servant of Kubera, who lusts after her. He assumes the form of Ugrasena and seduces her. Padmavati realizes the deception and prepares to curse him, but Gobhila argues about the duties of a pativrata (faithful wife). The chapter ends with a discourse on pativrata dharma.

beats.title: The Deception of Padmavati and the Birth of Kamsa
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Padmavati, a princess who is tricked by the demon Gobhila into leaving her husband King Ugrasena. She later gives birth to Kamsa, a powerful demon. The chapter also includes a sub-story about a woman named Sudheva who is abandoned by her family and eventually finds refuge near the Somnath temple. The narrative emphasizes the importance of chastity and the dangers of leaving one's husband.

beats.title: The Redemption of a Sinful Woman and the Power of Pativrata Dharma
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of a sinful woman who, after death, suffers in various hells and animal births. She is eventually reborn as a sow (shukari) and meets the virtuous queen Sudharma (or Sudharma's wife?), who, along with King Ikshvaku, helps her by transferring merit. The sow regains a divine form and goes to Vaikuntha. The chapter also includes the story of Sukala, a chaste wife who resists the advances of Indra and Kamadeva, and the power of pativrata dharma.

beats.title: The Chastity of Sukala
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of the chaste wife Sukala, who resists the advances of Indra and Kamadeva. Sukala's devotion to her husband and her spiritual wisdom protect her from temptation. Indra, after testing her, acknowledges her purity and advises Kamadeva to desist. The chapter emphasizes the power of truth, chastity, and the unity of the self.

beats.title: The Protection of Sukalā's Chastity
beats.summary: Satyā (Truth) and Dharma (Righteousness) discuss the threat posed by Kāma (Desire) and Indra to the chaste wife Sukalā. They devise a plan to protect her. Prajñā (Wisdom) is sent as a bird to Sukalā's home, foretelling her husband's return. Later, Kāma and Indra attempt to seduce Sukalā using a created forest and a disguised companion named Krīḍā (Play).

beats.title: The Power of a Chaste Wife
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of a chaste wife (Sukala) who resists the advances of Indra and Kamadeva, sent to test her fidelity. She boasts of her protection by virtues like truth and dharma, and threatens to burn them. Later, her husband Krikala returns from pilgrimage and learns from Dharma personified that his religious acts were fruitless because he left his wife alone. The chapter extols the virtue of a devoted wife and the importance of including her in religious rites.

beats.title: The Story of Krikala and Sukala, and the Penance of Pippala
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of Krikala and his wife Sukala, who are blessed by gods for their devotion and piety. It also includes the tale of Pippala's penance and Sukarma's filial piety, emphasizing the importance of serving parents and performing rituals with faith.

beats.title: The Pride of Pippala and the Wisdom of Sukarma
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the sage Pippala, who after severe penance receives a boon from the gods but becomes proud. A crane (actually Brahma) humbles him and directs him to Sukarma, the son of Kundala, who is a true knower of the ultimate reality. Sukarma demonstrates his power by summoning the gods, and Pippala learns about the nature of the Supreme Being, who is formless yet all-pervading.

beats.title: The Supreme Forms of Vishnu and the Glory of Parental Service
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the supreme form of Lord Vishnu, both the transcendental (parācīna) and the manifest (arvācīna) forms. It narrates the vision of sage Mārkaṇḍeya of Lord Vishnu lying on Śeṣanāga in the cosmic waters, and the appearance of the goddess Kālarātri. The chapter then shifts to a dialogue between the sage Pippala and a vidyādhara, where Pippala extols the virtue of serving one's parents, equating it to the highest spiritual practices and warning against neglect.

beats.title: Yayati and Matali's Dialogue on the Body and Soul
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of honoring parents, the dialogue between Yayati and Matali about the nature of the body and soul, and the description of Yayati's virtuous rule. It emphasizes that serving parents is superior to all rituals, and explains the impermanence of the physical body and the cycle of birth and death.

beats.title: Description of the Body and Embryology
beats.summary: This chapter describes the process of digestion, the formation of the body from food, the development of the fetus in the womb, the suffering of the soul in the womb, and the impure nature of the physical body. It includes details on the number of bones, muscles, hairs, and other bodily components, as well as the quantities of various bodily fluids.

beats.title: Discourse on the Impurity of the Body and the Sufferings of Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the inherent impurity of the human body, emphasizing that external purification is insufficient without inner purity. It describes the sufferings of life from birth through old age, including the pains of childhood, youth, and old age, and stresses the importance of cultivating detachment (vairagya) and inner purity through knowledge and good deeds. The chapter also mentions a dialogue between Yayati and his son, and concludes that those with pure hearts attain heaven and liberation.

beats.title: Dialogue on Suffering and Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the pervasive nature of suffering in all forms of existence, from human life to heavenly realms, and emphasizes the importance of detachment (vairagya) and knowledge (jnana) to attain liberation. It includes a dialogue between King Yayati and the sage Matali about the fruits of actions and the path to supreme peace.

beats.title: Discourse on Dharma and Sin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of honoring Brahmins, especially during Shraddha and sacred occasions. It details various sins equivalent to Brahmin-killing, including disrespecting gurus, stealing Brahmin property, and harming cows. It also lists upapapas (minor sins) and emphasizes the necessity of proper conduct towards guests, animals, and the righteous.

beats.title: Yayati and Matali Discuss Dharma and Adharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the consequences of theft and other sins, the role of Yama as the judge of the dead, the importance of charity (especially food and water), and the unity of Shiva and Vishnu. It also describes the rewards for various virtuous acts and the journey of the soul after death.

beats.title: King Yayati's Refusal of Heaven and Propagation of Vishnu's Name
beats.summary: King Yayati refuses to go to heaven, extols the power of Vishnu's name, and sends messengers to propagate Vaishnava dharma, leading to universal devotion.

beats.title: The Glory of Yayati's Reign and His Fall
beats.summary: This chapter describes the virtuous reign of King Yayati, during which all humans became devoted to Vishnu, free from old age and disease. Indra, jealous of Yayati's merit, sends Kamadeva and others to tempt him. Yayati falls into lust, loses his youth, and later regains it through his son Puru's sacrifice. Eventually, Yayati attains Vaikuntha with his queen and subjects.

beats.title: Yayati's Youth Exchange
beats.summary: King Yayati, while hunting, comes upon a beautiful lake and hears divine music. He finds a lovely woman named Ashrubindumati, daughter of Rati, and her friend Vishala, daughter of Varuna. Yayati desires Ashrubindumati, but Vishala says she will only accept him if he becomes young. Yayati asks his sons to exchange their youth for his old age. Turu and Yadu refuse and are cursed; Puru accepts. Yayati becomes young and enjoys with Ashrubindumati.

beats.title: Yayati's Dilemma and Devotion
beats.summary: King Yayati, married to Aśvabindumatī, promises to fulfill her wishes. She desires to see the celestial realms. Yayati hesitates, fearing that if he goes to heaven, mortals on earth will become immortal. After philosophical reflection on karma and destiny, he prays to Lord Vishnu for guidance.

beats.title: Yayati's Ascent to Vishnu's Abode
beats.summary: King Yayati, after ruling righteously, exchanges his old age for his son Puru's youth, then renounces the kingdom and travels with his wife and subjects through Indra's realm, Brahma's realm, and Shiva's realm, finally reaching Vishnu's abode, where he is honored by the gods.

beats.title: Yayati's Attainment of Vaikuntha and the Glory of Parents
beats.summary: This chapter describes King Yayati's journey to Vaikuntha, his vision of Lord Narayana, and the glorification of parents as supreme deities. It includes teachings on filial piety, the importance of serving mother and father, and the story of the sage Chyavana and the parrot Kapiñjala.

beats.title: Kujjala's Teaching on Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes a dialogue between the bird Kujjala and his son Ujjvala, where Kujjala teaches about the nature of the self (Atman) and two types of meditation on the Supreme Being: formless (nirākāra) and with form (sākāra). The formless meditation focuses on the attributeless, all-pervading consciousness, while the with-form meditation visualizes Lord Vishnu with specific features and ornaments.

beats.title: The Glory of Vishnu's Hundred Names
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Vishnu-shatanama-stotra (hundred names of Vishnu), including its ritual use, benefits, and the names themselves. It also includes a dialogue between sage Kunjala and his son Vijvala about karma and the results of good and bad deeds.

beats.title: Dialogue on Karma and Charity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the role of karma in determining one's experiences and rebirth. It emphasizes the importance of charity (dana), especially food donation, and contrasts the temporary nature of heaven with the eternal abode of Vishnu. The dialogue between King Subahu and the sage Jaimini highlights the superiority of devotion and yoga over mere ritualistic acts.

beats.title: The Discourse on Dharma and the Path to Heaven
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the merits of performing sacrifices and giving donations to Vishnu, and describes the types of actions that lead to hell or heaven. It includes a dialogue between King Subahu and the sage Jaimini about karma and its consequences, and ends with King Subahu's devotion to Vishnu and the recitation of the Shri Vasudevabhidhana Stotra.

beats.title: Hymn to Vāsudeva and Praṇava
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise (stuti) to Lord Vishnu, particularly in his form as Vāsudeva, and to the sacred syllable Om (Praṇava). The devotee repeatedly offers salutations and surrender to Vāsudeva, describing his various attributes, forms, and cosmic functions. The chapter emphasizes the power of Vishnu's feet, the sanctity of his foot-water, and the benefits of devotion. It also includes references to the Gayatri mantra and the Vedic tradition.

beats.title: The Slaying of Vihunda and the Weeping Maiden
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana contains hymns praising Lord Vasudeva (Vishnu), a dialogue between the bird Kujjala and his son Kapinjala about a weeping maiden on Mount Kailasa, and the story of the demon Vihunda who was slain by Vishnu after the gods sought refuge.

beats.title: The Demon Vihunda and the Kamoda Flowers
beats.summary: The demon king Vihunda, intent on killing the gods, encounters a beautiful woman (Maya) in the Nandana forest. She demands he worship Lord Shankara with seven crore lotus flowers from the Kamoda tree. Vihunda seeks advice from Shukracharya, who explains that Kamoda is a woman whose laughter produces fragrant flowers. Vihunda then meets Narada, who tricks him into thinking the flowers will come via the Ganges. Narada visits Kamoda and learns she is a manifestation of Vishnu's grace. The chapter also narrates the origin of Kamoda from the churning of the ocean and her future form as Tulsi.

beats.title: The Dream of Kamoda and the Demon Vihunda
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes a conversation between the sage Narada and a woman named Kamoda, who is distressed by a dream about Lord Vishnu's incarnation. Narada explains the types of dreams and reveals that Vishnu will incarnate due to a curse from sage Bhrigu. The story then shifts to a demon named Vihunda who disrupts Kamoda's worship of Shiva, but is ultimately killed by the goddess Parvati. The chapter also includes a sub-story about a bird named Kunjala who imparts wisdom to the sage Chyavana, discussing his past life as a Brahmin named Vidhyadhara.

beats.title: The Tale of Kungal and the Parrot
beats.summary: A sage named Kungal (or Kujjala) narrates his past life as a foolish son who refused to study, later met a Siddha who taught him the nature of knowledge (jnana), and through that teaching attained omniscience. He then explains how he was born as a parrot due to bad association, and how a parrot he kept was killed by a cat.

beats.title: The Story of King Vena and the Parrot's Past Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana (Bhumi Khanda) narrates the story of a sage who, after his pet parrot is killed by a cat, dies of grief and is reborn as a parrot due to his attachment. The parrot then recounts his past life and the importance of a guru as a moving tirtha. King Vena, after receiving teachings from Vishnu, performs an Ashvamedha yajna and attains Vishnu's abode. The chapter concludes with the glories of the Padma Purana and the five khandas.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation and Description of Bharatavarsha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the process of creation from primordial nature (Pradhana) through the evolution of Mahat, Ahankara, and the five elements, culminating in the formation of the cosmic egg (Hiranyagarbha) from which Brahma emerges as an avatar of Vishnu. It then lists the seven sacred mountains, numerous rivers, and various kingdoms (janapadas) of Bharatavarsha, including both Aryan and Mleccha territories.

beats.title: The Glorification of Pushkara Tirtha and Narmada River
beats.summary: This chapter describes the dialogue between King Yudhishthira and sage Narada, and within it, the dialogue between King Dilipa and sage Vasishtha, extolling the merits of pilgrimage, especially to Pushkara tirtha, and the glory of the Narmada river.

beats.title: Complete (part 166)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the sacred geography and spiritual merits associated with the Narmada River, particularly the Amarakantaka mountain and the confluence of the Narmada and Kaveri rivers. It narrates the story of Kubera obtaining a boon from Shiva at the Kaveri-Narmada sangam, and lists various tirthas along the Narmada's banks, extolling the benefits of bathing, fasting, and performing rituals there. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of the Narmada, which is said to have originated from Shiva's body.

beats.title: Description of Tirthas on the Banks of Narmada
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) along the banks of the Narmada River, detailing the benefits of bathing, worshipping, and performing rituals at each location. It emphasizes the purifying power of the Narmada and the various tirthas, including Shukabhadra, Bhimeshvara, Narmadeshvara, Adivyeshvara, Malkkishvara, Varuneshvara, Nirajeshvara, Kotishvara, Agastyeshvara, Ratistava, Indra-tirtha, Rishi-tirtha, Narada-tirtha, Deva-tirtha, Vamana-tirtha, Ekaneshvara, Vateshvara, Bhimeshvara, Varaneshvara, Soma-tirtha, Vyasa-tirtha, Erandi-tirtha, Skanda-tirtha, Angirasa-tirtha, Langala-tirtha, Vateshvara, Sarva-tirtha, Nada-Sangameshvara, Bhadra-tirtha, Angareshvara, Ayoni-Sangama, Pandaveshvara, Kambojakeshvara, and Chandrabhaga. The text also mentions the benefits of reciting a stotra and the importance of Kartik month observances.

beats.title: Glorification of Tirthas along Narmada
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various tirthas (holy sites) along the Narmada river, their significance, and the merits of performing rituals like bathing, donating cows, and fasting at these places. It includes a dialogue between Narada and Yudhishthira, and a story of Markandeya asking Shiva for a means to overcome worldly fear, leading to the glorification of Shankha Tirtha.

beats.title: Glory of Tirthas along Narmada
beats.summary: This chapter describes the merits of various tirthas (holy places) along the Narmada river, including Gayeshwar, Ganga-vadan, Dashashvamedhika, Bhrugu-tirtha, Gautameshvara, Dhautapapa, Hiranyadari, Kanakhala, Siddha-Janardana (Varaha-tirtha), Deva-tirtha, Rishi-tirtha, Narmadeshvara, Pitamaha-tirtha, Savitri-tirtha, Rudra-tirtha, Kratu-tirtha, Atavi, Kalavala Shrinkhala, Vimaleshvara, Keshini, and the confluence of Narmada and the sea. It explains the benefits of bathing, fasting, donating, and performing shraddha at these tirthas, leading to liberation from sins, attainment of various lokas (worlds), and ultimately moksha. The chapter also highlights the importance of reciting or hearing this chapter.

beats.title: Tirtha Yatra and Their Merits
beats.summary: This chapter describes a series of sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites) and the benefits of visiting them, including bathing, worship, and offerings. It emphasizes the purifying power of these sites and the rewards such as attaining various lokas (worlds) and the fruits of sacrifices like Ashvamedha and Rajasuya.

beats.title: Glories of Tirthas in Kurukshetra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of various tirthas (sacred sites) in the region of Kurukshetra, including Manush-tirtha, Brahma-anusvara-tirtha, Pundarika-tirtha, Trivishtapa, Mishraka, Rinanta-kupa, Vedi-tirtha, Mrigadhuma, Koti-tirtha, Vamana-tirtha, Kulampuna, Rakshihotra, Shrikunja, Pushkara, Kapalamochana, Prithudaka, Uddalaka, Sahasraka, Renuka, Vimochana, Panchata, Sthanu-vata, Ekaratri, Tejorashi, and Sannihita. It details the benefits of bathing, fasting, donating, and worshipping at these sites, including liberation from sins, attainment of heaven, and fruits of various sacrifices.

beats.title: The Story of Hemakundala
