beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the glory of Dharmatirtha and Kurukshetra, describes the merits of bathing in the Yamuna, and narrates the story of the wealthy merchant Hemakundala who renounced his wealth for spiritual pursuits.

beats.title: The Fate of Two Sinful Brothers
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana (Svargakhanda) describes the glory of Dharmatirtha and the deeds that lead to heaven or hell. It narrates the story of two brothers who lived a sinful life, died, and were judged by Yama: one sent to hell, the other to heaven due to a single virtuous act (bathing in the Kachchhindi river during Magha). The chapter emphasizes ahimsa (non-violence) as the highest dharma and details the consequences of violence and compassion.

beats.title: Glory of Dharma Tirtha and Path to Heaven
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana (Khargakhanda 1) describes the glory of Dharma Tirtha and the auspicious and inauspicious actions that lead to heaven or hell. It emphasizes the importance of daily worship, building wells and ponds, bathing, charity, truthfulness, non-anger, celibacy, and devotion to Vishnu. It also extols the purifying power of the Ganges, pranayama, and the protection of women's chastity.

beats.title: The Glories of Vishnu Worship and Shalagrama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the virtues of devotion to Vishnu, especially through the worship of the Shalagrama stone, the chanting of the twelve-syllable mantra, and the observance of Ekadashi. It emphasizes that even great sinners like Ajamila can attain liberation by calling upon Narayana, and that the company of Vaishnavas purifies all. The chapter also warns against criticizing Vaishnavas and describes the benefits of donating Shalagrama stones.

beats.title: The Power of Serving Saints
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Vikuṇḍala, who meets a divine messenger (devadūta) and learns about the power of serving saintly persons. The messenger recounts Vikuṇḍala's past life as a householder who hosted four enlightened sannyāsīs, earning immense merit. Vikuṇḍala transfers that merit to his brother suffering in hell, freeing him. The chapter also describes various tīrthas and their benefits.

beats.title: Glory of Tirthas and Varanasi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the merits of various tirthas (holy places), especially Varanasi (Kashi) and the Avimukta kshetra. It details the fruits of pilgrimages, baths, fasts, and donations, including the benefits of visiting specific tirthas like Ashvamedha, Bhṛgutadkh, Vīrapramokṣa, Kṛttikā, Maghā, Sandhyā-tīrtha, Vidyā-tīrtha, Mahāśrama, Vetasikā, Brāhmaṇikā, Sugandha, Naimiṣa, Gajendheda, Bāhudā, Sarayū, Gomati, Rāmatīrtha, Jatasāhasra, Ūrdhvasthāna, Kārttikeya, Kapilākuṇḍa, and Kālī. The chapter also narrates a dialogue between Lord Shiva and Parvati extolling the supremacy of Varanasi, where even sinners attain liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Kapardishvara and the Liberation of a Pishacha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana glorifies the city of Varanasi (Kashi) and the Kapardishvara linga. It describes the story of the sage Shankukarna who, through devotion to Kapardishvara and bathing in the Pishachamochana kunda, liberates a pishacha (ghost) who was a former Brahmin. The chapter emphasizes the power of Varanasi to grant liberation and the importance of worshiping Kapardishvara.

beats.title: Merits of Tirthas in Gaya and Beyond
beats.summary: This chapter describes the merits of various tirthas (pilgrimage sites) in Gaya and other regions, including the benefits of bathing, fasting, and performing rituals such as pindadana. It lists specific tirthas like Brahma Sarovar, Dharmaprishtha, Kapila's footprints, and others, and the rewards such as attaining the fruits of Ashvamedha and Rajasuya sacrifices, liberation from sins, and reaching various heavenly realms. The chapter also includes hymns to Brahmapara and Vishnu.

beats.title: The Glories of Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the merits of visiting various tirthas (sacred sites) in India, especially along the Ganges and other rivers, and the fruits of performing rituals like bathing, fasting, and offering oblations. It highlights the supreme importance of Prayaga (confluence of Ganga and Yamuna) and the Ganges in the Kali Yuga.

beats.title: The Glory of Tirthas and the Consolation of Yudhishthira
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of tirthas (holy places), especially the Ganga and Prayaga. It narrates how King Dilipa and later Yudhishthira performed tirtha-yatra, and includes teachings on the merit of bathing in the Ganga, visiting Prayaga, and the importance of tirtha-seva. The sage Markandeya consoles Yudhishthira after the Mahabharata war and extols the virtues of Prayaga.

beats.title: Glory of Prayaga and Other Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Prayaga (the confluence of Ganga and Yamuna) and various tirthas in the region. It explains the benefits of bathing, giving donations, and dying at these holy places, emphasizing the importance of avoiding vehicles and accepting gifts. The chapter also mentions the Akshayavata (banyan tree) and the presence of deities like Vishnu, Shiva, and Indra.

beats.title: The Glorification of Prayaga and the Yamuna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the greatness of the Ganga and Yamuna rivers, especially at Prayaga (confluence). It describes the purifying power of bathing, drinking, and dying at these sacred waters, and emphasizes that Prayaga is superior to all other tirthas. The sage Markandeya explains to Yudhishthira the merits of various tirthas along the Yamuna and the supreme importance of Prayaga for liberation.

beats.title: The Greatness of Prayaga and the Power of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the greatness of Prayaga (confluence of Ganga and Yamuna), its tirthas, and the importance of devotion to Vishnu/Hari. It includes a dialogue between Yudhishthira and Markandeya, and later between Krishna and Yudhishthira, emphasizing that bhakti (devotion) surpasses all rituals and tirthas. The chapter also highlights the power of Hari's name, image, and tulasi.

beats.title: The Glory of the Holy Name
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the supreme power of chanting the name of Krishna (Hari). It declares that the name of Krishna is the greatest purifier, surpassing all holy rivers and pilgrimages. The text emphasizes that even a great sinner can be liberated by chanting the holy name. It also stresses the unity of Vishnu and Shiva, warning against making distinctions between them. The chapter describes the glory of devotion, the importance of chanting, and the futility of worldly lamentation without devotion. It concludes with a dialogue where sages ask Suta about the practice of Karma Yoga, and Suta recounts the teachings of Vyasa.

beats.title: Duties of a Brahmacārī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the duties and rules for a brahmacārī (celibate student), including the sacred thread (yajñopavīta), girdle (mekhalā), staff (daṇḍa), and the proper conduct towards gurus (teachers), parents, and elders. It emphasizes the importance of daily rituals like sandhyā (twilight worship), homa (fire sacrifice), and tarpana (libations). The chapter also details the correct method of pronouncing blessings (āśīrvāda) and the hierarchy of respect among gurus.

beats.title: Conduct and Purification Rules
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the proper conduct for brahmins and householders, including rules for greeting, respect based on age and knowledge, the duties of a brahmachari (celibate student) regarding begging for alms, and detailed instructions for achamana (sipping water for purification). It emphasizes the importance of humility, self-control, and ritual purity.

beats.title: Rules of Purity and Brahmachari Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana provides detailed instructions on purity, bodily functions, and the conduct of a brahmachari (celibate student) towards his guru. It covers rules for touching objects, eating, urination and defecation, and proper behavior in the guru's presence.

beats.title: Duties of a Student and Rules for Vedic Study
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the duties of a student (brahmachari) towards his guru, the importance of daily study of the Vedas and Puranas, the significance of the Gayatri mantra, and rules regarding when Vedic study should be suspended (anadhyaya). It also lists ten types of persons worthy of respect and six types of disciples eligible for instruction.

beats.title: Duties of Brahmachari and Grihastha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the duties of a brahmachari (celibate student) and a grihastha (householder). It emphasizes the importance of Vedic study, proper conduct, and the performance of prescribed rituals. The text also describes the qualities of a virtuous brahmin and the path to liberation through knowledge and righteous living.

beats.title: Duties of a Householder
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the duties and conduct of a householder (grihastha), emphasizing daily rituals, truthfulness, control of anger, charity, and devotion to the Vedas. It warns against association with impure persons, theft, slander, and disrespect towards gods, gurus, and the Vedas. The chapter also describes the characteristics of a true Brahmana and the consequences of sinful actions.

beats.title: Conduct and Prohibitions for a Virtuous Life
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana provides a list of prohibitions and guidelines for proper conduct, especially for Brahmins and householders. It covers topics such as avoiding criticism of guru, deities, and scriptures; not looking at certain things or people in specific states; refraining from various actions like eating with one's wife, stepping on sacred items, and disrespecting cows, Brahmins, and fire. It also warns against false accusations, anger, greed, and other vices, and emphasizes purity and respect in daily life.

beats.title: Discourse on Forbidden Food and Charity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses forbidden foods and persons whose food should not be accepted, the consequences of eating such food, and the merits of various types of charity (dana) including land, food, and knowledge. It also describes the proper recipients of charity and the benefits of giving.

beats.title: Duties and Merits of Charity and Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of charity (dana) and worship, especially on specific lunar days like Krishna Chaturdashi, Ashtami, and Amavasya. It emphasizes giving to Brahmins, the benefits of various types of donations (water, food, cows, etc.), and the duties of householders and vanaprasthas. The chapter also warns against greed and improper acceptance of gifts.

beats.title: Duties of Vanaprastha and Sannyasa Ashramas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the duties and practices of the vanaprastha (forest-dweller) and sannyasa (renunciate) ashramas. It details the lifestyle, rituals, and spiritual disciplines for those in the third and fourth stages of life, emphasizing detachment, austerity, and devotion to Brahman.

beats.title: Rules for Sannyasis
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the rules and practices for a sannyasi (renunciant). It covers detachment, non-violence, truthfulness, celibacy, meditation on Brahman, and specific vows like the sotapana and kricchra penances. The chapter emphasizes the importance of controlling the mind, performing pranayama, and meditating on Vishnu as the supreme self to attain liberation.

beats.title: Discourse on Dharma and Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the gravity of theft, penances for various sins of ascetics, the nature of Mahadeva (Shiva) as the supreme Brahman, the importance of devotion to Hari (Vishnu) in Kali Yuga, and the dangers of women's allurements. It also recounts that Vyasa taught ashrama-dharma to sages.

beats.title: Discourse on Renunciation of Women and Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the impurity of the female body and the dangers of attachment to women, advocating for devotion to Lord Vishnu (Govinda, Janardana) and service to Brahmins as the path to liberation.

beats.title: Glorification of Bhakti and the Ganges
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana emphasizes the importance of devotion to Vishnu/Krishna, the sanctity of the Ganges, the value of Puranic stories, and the futility of worldly attachments. It describes the cycle of birth and death and advocates for bhakti as the means to liberation.

beats.title: The Puranic Body of Vishnu and the Glory of Padma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Puranic body of Lord Vishnu, where various Puranas are identified as parts of His body. It extols the glory of the Padma Purana, especially its Svargakhanda, and recounts the beginning of the story of Rama's Ashvamedha sacrifice, as narrated by Suta to the sages and by Sesha to Vatsyayana.

beats.title: श्रीराम का अयोध्या लौटना और भरत से मिलन
beats.summary: भगवान श्रीराम के अयोध्या लौटने के मार्ग का वर्णन है। विभीषण उनके साथ जाने को उत्सुक हैं। मार्ग में सीता को आश्रम, तीर्थ, मुनि आदि दिखाए जाते हैं। नन्दिग्राम में भरत का वियोग-दुःख और तपस्या का वर्णन है। हनुमान भरत को सूचना देते हैं। भरत श्रीराम से मिलते हैं और दुःख प्रकट करते हैं।

beats.title: श्रीराम का अयोध्या आगमन और राज्याभिषेक
beats.summary: भरतजी श्रीराम के चरणों में गिरकर रोते हैं और अयोध्या को सुनसान देखकर हर्षित होते हैं। श्रीराम का नगर प्रवेश, माताओं से मिलना, राज्य ग्रहण और रामराज्य की व्यवस्था का वर्णन है। सुमुख नगर की सजावट का प्रबंध करता है। श्रीराम पुष्पक विमान से अयोध्या आते हैं, माता कैकेयी, सुमित्रा और कौसल्या से मिलते हैं। सीता का पातिव्रत्य और भरत का सम्मान दर्शाया गया है।

beats.title: Coronation of Rama and the Boon to the Gods
beats.summary: This chapter describes the coronation of Lord Rama, the praise of Rama by the gods, Rama granting them a boon, and the description of Rama's righteous reign (Ram Rajya). It also includes the blessings of Queen Kausalya to Sita and the happiness of the people during Rama's rule.

beats.title: The Glory of Ram Rajya and the Lineage of Ravana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the idyllic state of Rama's kingdom (Ram Rajya), where prosperity, righteousness, and happiness prevail. It then narrates the arrival of sage Agastya in Rama's court, Rama's inquiry about the identities of Ravana and Kumbhakarna, and Agastya's account of their lineage: born to sage Vishrava and Kaikesi, with Kubera as their half-brother.

beats.title: The Origin of Ravana and the Promise of Vishnu's Incarnation
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Ravana's birth, his mother's jealousy towards Kubera, Ravana's determination to surpass Kubera through severe penance, his obtaining boons from Brahma, his subsequent tyranny over the gods, and the gods' plea to Vishnu for help. Vishnu promises to incarnate as Rama to kill Ravana, and the gods take birth as monkeys and bears to assist him.

beats.title: Rama's Ashvamedha Sacrifice
beats.summary: Agastya advises Rama to perform the Ashvamedha sacrifice, describing the ritual details including horse selection, protection, and the king's duties. Rama prepares the sacrifice, invites sages, and after their arrival, discussions on dharma for various varnas and ashramas take place.

beats.title: Preparation for the Ashvamedha Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the duties of a householder, including offerings to deities, ancestors, and humans, and various prohibitions regarding food, conduct, and auspicious days. It then narrates the preparation for an Ashvamedha sacrifice by Lord Rama, following the advice of sage Vasishtha. The horse is released, and the sacrificial setup is described with sages assigned to guard the eight gates.

beats.title: The Release of the Sacrificial Horse and Instructions for Its Protection
beats.summary: In the Padma Purana, Patala Khanda, the chapter describes the release of the sacrificial horse for the Ashvamedha yajna by King Rama, its protection by Shatrughna, and the instructions given by Rama and Vasishtha regarding the horse's protection and the conduct of warriors. The horse is adorned and sent forth with a large army, including vanaras and other warriors. Rama entrusts Hanuman and others with the task of protecting Shatrughna and the horse.

beats.title: अश्वमेध यज्ञ का घोड़ा और शत्रुघ्न का अभियान
beats.summary: शोषजी कहते हैं कि श्रीरामचन्द्रजी ने मन्त्री सुमन्त्र से पूछा कि अश्वमेध यज्ञ के घोड़े की रक्षा के लिए किन-किन वीरों को नियुक्त किया जाए। सुमन्त्र ने प्रतापाम्रय, नीररल, रक्ष्मीनिधि, रिपुताप, उग्राश्च और इासख्रवित् जैसे राजाओं के नाम बताए। श्रीराम ने उन्हें आज्ञा दी। वे सब कवच धारण कर शत्रुघ्न के निवास पर गए। श्रीराम ने ऋत्विजों को दक्षिणा देकर पूजन किया। यज्ञ में दान-पुण्य का क्रम चल रहा था। शत्रुघ्न ने माता से आज्ञा लेकर घोड़े की रक्षा के लिए प्रस्थान किया। पुष्कल ने अपनी पत्नी कान्तिमती से विदा ली और शत्रुघ्न की सेना में शामिल हुआ। वे घोड़े के साथ पाञ्चाल, कुरु, उत्तरकुरु, दशार्ण आदि देशों में भ्रमण करते रहे। लोग श्रीराम की कीर्ति गाते थे। सुमति नामक सचिव भी साथ था। अनेक राजा शत्रुघ्न के चरणों में गिरकर अपनी सम्पत्ति श्रीराम को समर्पित करते थे। अंत में वे अहिच्छत्र नगरी पहुँचे।

beats.title: King Sumada's Penance and the Goddess Kamaksha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the city of Chhatra, ruled by King Sumada, and the temple of Goddess Kamaksha. It narrates King Sumada's intense twelve-year penance on Hemakuta mountain, his temptation by Indra's apsaras and Kamadeva, his steadfast devotion, and the eventual appearance of the goddess who grants him a boon.

beats.title: Sumada's Devotion and Meeting with Shatrughna
beats.summary: King Sumada of Ahichchhatra, a devotee of Goddess Kamaksha, receives a boon that he will meet Shatrughna and serve Lord Rama's horse. When the horse arrives, Sumada welcomes Shatrughna, offers his kingdom, and accompanies him. They travel, hearing sages praise Rama, and eventually reach the hermitage of Chyavana where Sumati narrates the story of Chyavana's marriage to Sukanya.

beats.title: The Story of Chyavana and Sukanya
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Sage Chyavana, his birth from Sage Bhrigu's wife after a demon attack, his penance, and his encounter with King Sharyati's daughter Sukanya. It also includes the background of how Chyavana later gave the Ashwini Kumaras a share in sacrifices, angering Indra.

beats.title: Sukanya and Chyavana
beats.summary: King Sharyati gives his daughter Sukanya in marriage to the blind sage Chyavana to appease his anger. Sukanya serves Chyavana devotedly for a thousand years. The Ashvini Kumaras visit, and Sukanya asks them to restore her husband's youth. They agree on condition that Chyavana gives them a share in sacrifices. Chyavana enters a pond and emerges young, identical to the Ashvins. Later, Chyavana performs a Soma sacrifice and offers Soma to the Ashvins, angering Indra. Chyavana paralyzes Indra's arm, then relents. The story highlights Sukanya's devotion and Chyavana's power.

beats.title: Shatrughna's Journey with the Sacrificial Horse
beats.summary: Shatrughna and Sumati discuss the glory of Lord Rama. The sacrificial horse arrives at Sage Chyavana's ashram. Shatrughna meets Chyavana, who praises Rama's virtues. Chyavana becomes ecstatic in devotion. Shatrughna invites Chyavana to the sacrifice. Hanuman carries Chyavana to Rama. Later, Shatrughna continues with the horse, meets King Vimara who submits, and then sees a wondrous mountain. Sumati describes the mountain's qualities and who cannot see it.

beats.title: The Glory of Nilagiri and King Ratnagriva
beats.summary: The chapter describes the glory of the Nilagiri mountain and the deity Purushottama residing there. It narrates the story of King Ratnagriva who, desiring liberation, is advised by a sage to worship Lord Ramachandra. The sage recounts his pilgrimage to various holy places and the miraculous sight of four-armed beings on Nilagiri, emphasizing the mountain's power to grant salvation.

beats.title: The Tribal Boy Who Became Four-Armed
beats.summary: A Brahmin narrates to King Ratgriva the story of a tribal boy who attained a four-armed form like Vishnu by eating prasad at a temple on Nilachala hill. The Brahmin then describes the glory of pilgrimage and the importance of devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: King Ralagriva's Pilgrimage to Purushottama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the pilgrimage to Purushottama (Jagannatha) undertaken by King Ralagriva. It details the proper method of pilgrimage, the benefits of visiting holy places, and the journey itself, including the encounter with the Gandaki river (also called Shalagrami or Narayani) and its origin from Brahma's cheek.

beats.title: The Glory of Shalagrama Shila and the Liberation of the Pulkasa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the glory of the Shalagrama shila (sacred stone) and its worship. It describes the stone as a direct manifestation of Lord Vishnu, explains the rules for its worship (including prohibitions for women and certain numbers), and narrates the story of a sinful Pulkasa (hunter) who was saved from hell by a Vaishnava who gave him Vishnu's charanamrita and touched him with a Shalagrama shila, leading to his liberation by Vishnu's attendants.

beats.title: The Glory of Shalagrama and Purushottama Darshana
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a Pulkasa (outcaste) who, through the touch of a Shalagrama shila and association with a Vaishnava, is freed from Yama's noose and attains heaven. It also describes King Ratnamrivaka's pilgrimage to Purushottama Kshetra, his worship of Shalagrama shilas, and his eventual vision of Lord Purushottama after intense penance at the confluence of Ganga and the ocean.

beats.title: King Ratnagriva's Liberation
beats.summary: King Ratnagriva, guided by a sage, worships Lord Purushottama on Mount Nilagiri. He performs rituals, offers a hymn of praise, and receives the Lord's grace, ultimately attaining a four-armed form and liberation to Vaikuntha.

beats.title: The Glory of Nilagiri and the Ashvamedha Horse
beats.summary: The chapter describes the journey of King Ratnagriva and his companions to Vaikuntha after receiving the grace of Lord Purushottama. It also narrates the story of the horse of the ashvamedha yajna being captured by Prince Damana, leading to a battle with King Pratapagra. The chapter highlights the glory of Nilagiri and the power of the Lord's name.

beats.title: The Battle of Daman and Pushkal
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle between Prince Daman (son of King Subahu) and King Pratapamraja, followed by the intervention of Pushkal (son of Bharata) on behalf of Shatrughna. Daman defeats Pratapamraja and captures the sacrificial horse. Pushkal then engages Daman in a duel using various divine weapons (Agneya, Varuna, Vayavya, Parvatasra, Vajra). The chapter ends with Pushkal using the Vajra weapon to shatter the mountains created by Daman's Parvatasra.

beats.title: Battle of the Krauncha Formation
beats.summary: The chapter describes a battle where King Subahu's son Damana is wounded by Shatrughna's nephew Pushkala. Subahu and his forces form a Krauncha formation. Lakshminidhi, son of King Janaka, leads an attack to break the formation, confronting Suketu.

beats.title: The Battle of Subahu and Shatrughna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle between King Subahu's forces and the army of Shatrughna. Key events include the duel between Lakshminidhi and Suketu, the death of Prince Chitrang at the hands of Pushkala, and the subsequent grief of King Subahu. The chapter ends with Subahu's sons Vichitra and Daman entering the battle.

beats.title: Subahu's Surrender
beats.summary: King Subahu, after being kicked by Hanuman, has a vision of Lord Rama performing a sacrifice in Ayodhya. He realizes Rama's divine nature, repents, and decides to surrender his kingdom and the sacrificial horse to Shatrughna. His sons agree, and they all go to Shatrughna, offering their allegiance.

beats.title: The Story of King Satyavan and the Glory of Cow Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of King Satyavan of Tejahpura, his devotion to Lord Rama, and the importance of cow worship. It includes the narrative of King Ritambhara who, desiring a son, is advised by sage Jabali to worship cows. The chapter also recounts King Janaka's journey after death and the liberation of sinners by his presence.

beats.title: King Janaka at the Gates of Hell
beats.summary: King Janaka, while on a divine plane, approaches the gates of hell out of compassion for the suffering beings. Yama (Dharmaraja) explains that only sinners come there and questions Janaka's presence. Janaka refuses to leave unless the sinners are freed. Yama describes the specific sins of various individuals and their punishments. Janaka asks how they can be liberated, and Yama suggests Janaka transfer his merit from uttering Rama's name. Janaka gives away his lifetime's merit, freeing the sinners. Yama then reveals that Janaka's own minor sin (obstructing a cow) caused him to see hell, but he is now purified.

beats.title: The Story of King Ritambhara and Satyavan
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of King Ritambhara and his son Satyavan, who through devotion to cows and Lord Rama attain liberation. It includes instructions on cow worship, the appearance of Shatrughna, the demon Vidyunmali stealing the horse, and the subsequent battle.

beats.title: The Vows and Battle Preparations
beats.summary: This chapter describes the preparations for battle against a demon who stole the horse of Lord Rama. Various warriors, including Pushkala, Hanuman, and Shatrughna, make vows to defeat the demon. Pushkala initially wounds the demon Vidyunmali, but is struck down by the demon's brother Ugra. Hanuman then attacks, destroying many demons and breaking Ugra's trident. Ugra uses dark magic to create chaos, but the outcome is left unresolved.

beats.title: Shatrughna's Victory and the Sage's Teaching
beats.summary: Shatrughna defeats the demon Vidyunmali and his forces, recovers the sacrificial horse, and visits the sage Aranyaka. The sage teaches the supremacy of Shri Rama's worship over all other practices.

beats.title: The Glory and Meditation of Lord Rama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the meditation and worship of Lord Rama, including a detailed visualization of his divine form. It recounts Rama's life story from his incarnation to key events like killing Tadaka, redeeming Ahalya, breaking Shiva's bow, marrying Sita, and going into exile. The sage Lomasha narrates these events to Aranyaka, emphasizing that devotion to Rama is the supreme path to liberation.

beats.title: The Ramayana War
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the events of the Ramayana from the exile of Rama, Sita, and Lakshmana, through the abduction of Sita by Ravana, the search for Sita, the building of the bridge to Lanka, the great war, and the eventual victory of Rama over Ravana, including the coronation of Vibhishana and Sita's agni pariksha.

beats.title: Rama's Return and Ashvamedha Yajna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the return of Lord Rama to Ayodhya after defeating Ravana, his coronation, and the beginning of his reign. It details the Ashvamedha yajna (horse sacrifice) performed by Rama, the journey of the sacrificial horse to the hermitage of sage Aranyaka, and the interactions between the sage, Hanuman, and other characters. The chapter also includes reflections on devotion to Rama and the power of his story.

beats.title: The Liberation of Aranyaka Muni and the Wandering Horse
beats.summary: The chapter describes the meeting between King Shri Ramachandra and the sage Aranyaka Muni. The sage visits Ramachandra's court, is honored, and later narrates the power of Rama's name. He then attains liberation (sayujya-mukti) by merging into Rama's form. The chapter also introduces the horse of the Ashvamedha sacrifice wandering to the city of Devapura, where King Viramani rules, and his son Rukmangada captures the horse.

beats.title: अश्वापहरण और युद्ध की तैयारी
beats.summary: राजा वीरमणि के पुत्र रुक्माङ्गद ने श्रीराम के अश्वमेध यज्ञ के घोड़े का अपहरण कर लिया। वीरमणि ने शिव से सलाह ली, जिन्होंने घोड़ा लौटाने की सलाह दी, पर वीरमणि ने युद्ध का निश्चय किया। शत्रुघ्न सेना के साथ आए और नारद ने उन्हें सूचित किया कि घोड़ा देवपुर के राजा वीरमणि के पुत्र ने लिया है। युद्ध की तैयारियाँ हुईं।

beats.title: Battle of Virmani and Shatrughna's Forces
beats.summary: King Virmani attacks with a jeweled chariot. Shatrughna consults Sumati, then engages in battle. Pushkala fights Rukmangada, using divine weapons. Hanuman advises Pushkala. King Virmani and Pushkala duel; Virmani's army is destroyed. Hanuman defeats Virasimha, and Virmani surrenders.

beats.title: The Great Battle and Divine Intervention
beats.summary: The chapter describes a fierce battle where Pushkala fights with arrows, then remembers Shri Raghunathji and shoots a third arrow that wounds the king. The king's army flees and Pushkara is victorious. Hanumanji defeats Virasimha, Virbhadra kills Pushkala, and Shankarji causes Shatrughna to faint. Hanuman's prowess revives the dead warriors, and Shri Rama appears. Virmani surrenders. The narrative details various duels and divine interventions.

beats.title: Hanuman's Battle with Shiva's Ganas and the Revival of Warriors
beats.summary: Hanuman fights Shiva's ganas, defeats Nandi, and requests a boon from Shiva to protect fallen warriors. He goes to Dronagiri for the sanjivani herb, encounters guardians, and returns. Shatrughna uses various astras, revives warriors, and ultimately Rama appears, resolving the conflict.

beats.title: The Stiffening of the Sacrificial Horse and Its Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Viramani, who performed severe penance at the Mahakala temple in Ujjain, leading to Shiva granting him a boon. Later, during Rama's Ashvamedha sacrifice, the horse enters a garden where a Brahmin cursed to be a demon causes its body to stiffen. Shatrughna seeks help from the sage Shonaka, who reveals the cause and the eventual liberation through Rama's story.

beats.title: Naraka Varnana and Liberation through Rama Katha
beats.summary: The chapter describes various hells (narakas) for different sins, the importance of hearing Lord Rama's story and Hari kirtan for liberation, and concludes with the story of a Brahmin who became a demon but was freed by listening to Rama's glories.

beats.title: The Capture of the Sacrificial Horse and the Test of King Suratha
beats.summary: The chapter describes the journey of the sacrificial horse of Rama, which is captured by King Suratha. Suratha, a devoted follower of Rama, prepares for battle. Yama, disguised as a sage, tests Suratha's devotion by praising karma and criticizing Rama, but Suratha rebukes him and remains steadfast in his faith.

beats.title: King Suratha's Devotion and the Battle for the Horse
beats.summary: Yama appears to King Suratha, grants him a boon that he will not die until he meets Rama. Suratha's servants capture the sacrificial horse of Shatrughna. Angada is sent as a messenger to Suratha, who refuses to return the horse unless Rama himself comes. Suratha prepares for battle. Angada returns and reports to Shatrughna. The battle begins, with Suratha defeating Hanuman and others, but Rama's arrival saves everyone.

beats.title: The Battle of Pushkala and Champaka
beats.summary: In this chapter, the battle between King Suratha's forces and Shatrughna's army intensifies. Pushkala fights Champaka, who reveals himself as a devotee of Rama named Ramadasa. Champaka binds Pushkala with a divine weapon. Hanuman intervenes, defeats Champaka, and frees Pushkala. King Suratha confronts Hanuman, who remains steadfast in his devotion to Rama.

beats.title: The Battle of King Suratha with Rama's Forces
beats.summary: In this chapter of the Padma Purana, King Suratha fights against the army of Lord Rama, including Hanuman, Shatrughna, Sugriva, and others. Suratha defeats many warriors using divine weapons, but ultimately all are bound by Rama's weapon. The bound warriors pray to Rama for release, and the chapter ends with a call to worship Lord Krishna.

beats.title: Hanuman's Release and Lav's Capture of the Horse
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of King Suratha, who is bound by Hanuman. Hanuman prays to Lord Rama for release. Rama arrives with Lakshmana and Bharata, frees Hanuman and revives the fallen warriors. Suratha offers his kingdom to Rama, who stays for three days. Later, the chapter describes how Lav, son of Rama and Sita, captures the sacrificial horse at Valmiki's ashram, cutting the arms of the guards. The chapter also includes a flashback to Sita's pregnancy and Rama's inquiry about her wishes.

beats.title: The Banishment of Sita
beats.summary: This chapter describes how Rama sends spies to Ayodhya to gauge public opinion after hearing gossip about Sita. The spies overhear various citizens praising Rama and his deeds, but also encounter a washerman who criticizes Sita, leading Rama to decide to banish her to the forest.

beats.title: Sita's Exile
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the episode of Sita's exile. Spies report to Rama that a washerman criticized Sita for living in Ravana's house, leading Rama to order Sita's abandonment. Bharata protests, but Rama insists due to public opinion. The chapter also includes a backstory of a parrot couple who foretold Rama and Sita's marriage, and Sita's attempt to capture them.

beats.title: Sita's Inquiry and the Washerman's Tale
beats.summary: Birds tell Sita about Rama and Valmiki's Ramayana. Sita questions them, and a female bird describes Rama's beauty. The birds reveal the story of a washerman whose past life led to Sita's exile. Shatrughna faints upon hearing Rama's decision to abandon Sita.

beats.title: The Abandonment of Sita
beats.summary: Lakshmana is ordered by Rama to abandon Sita in the forest due to public scandal. Despite Lakshmana's reluctance, he obeys and takes Sita to the forest, where she learns of her abandonment and faints.

beats.title: Sita's Abandonment and Refuge with Valmiki
beats.summary: Sita, abandoned by Rama at the behest of a washerman, falls unconscious. Lakshmana revives her, and she laments her fate, questioning Rama's actions. She instructs Lakshmana to return to Rama and convey her message to her mothers-in-law. After Lakshmana leaves, Sita faints again. The sage Valmiki finds her, consoles her, and takes her to his ashram, where she resides, devoted to Rama.

beats.title: The Birth and Exploits of Lava and Kusha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth and upbringing of Lava and Kusha, the twin sons of Sita and Rama, in Valmiki's hermitage. It details their education, including the Vedas and Dhanurveda, and their training as warriors. The narrative then shifts to the arrival of Shatrughna's army during the Ashvamedha horse sacrifice. Lava defeats the army, kills the commander Kalajit, and demonstrates his martial prowess.

beats.title: Lava's Battle with Shatrughna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle between Lava and Shatrughna's forces. Lava defeats Shatrughna's army, including the general Kalajit, and later fights Shatrughna himself. Hanuman is also involved but is defeated by Lava. The chapter ends with Shatrughna questioning Lava's identity.

beats.title: Kusha and Lava's Battle with Shatrughna
beats.summary: Kusha and Lava fight King Shatrughna's army. Lava is captured, then Kusha rescues him. They reveal their identity as sons of Sita and Rama. A battle ensues between Kusha and Shatrughna, with Kusha using the Narayana astra which fails against Shatrughna.

beats.title: Kura's Victory and the Revelation of Rama's Horse
beats.summary: This chapter describes the battle between Kura and the forces of Rama, including Raturgha, Suratha, Hanuman, and Sugriva. Kura defeats them one by one, captures the horse of the Ashvamedha yajna, and binds Hanuman and Sugriva. Later, Kura and his brother Kava take the captives to their mother, who reveals that the horse belongs to Rama, their father, and orders them to release the captives. The sons argue that their actions were according to kshatriya dharma.

beats.title: The Return of the Horse and the Meeting at Ayodhya
beats.summary: Sita revives the slain army through her truthfulness. Shatrughna and Pushkala release the horse and return to Ayodhya. King Suratha and others meet Rama. Sumati narrates the horse's journey, including encounters with King Sumada, King Subahu, and a boy at Valmiki's ashram who defeats Shatrughna.

beats.title: The Reunion of Rama and Sita
beats.summary: King Shatrughna becomes angry and faints a hero. Another warrior appears, defeats the army, and takes the horses and ornaments. Hanuman and another monkey are captured. The sage Valmiki revives the army and reveals the identity of Rama's sons. Rama learns about his sons and Sita's purity. Valmiki narrates how Sita was abandoned, gave birth to twins, and how they were educated. The twins sing the Ramayana, impressing gods. Valmiki advises Rama to accept Sita and his sons. Rama sends Lakshmana to bring Sita back.

beats.title: Sita's Refusal and Rama's Message
beats.summary: Sita questions Lakshmana about the well-being of Rama and others, and Lakshmana conveys Rama's message explaining that all events are governed by destiny (adrishta). Rama expresses his love for Sita and asks her to return to Ayodhya. Sita, however, decides to stay in the forest to perform penance and sends her sons with Lakshmana. Lakshmana returns to Rama, who sends him back with further words. Valmiki instructs the boys to sing Rama's glories.

beats.title: Composition of the Ramayana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the composition of the Ramayana by Valmiki. It narrates how Valmiki, witnessing a hunter kill a krauncha bird, spontaneously uttered a curse that became the first shloka. Later, Brahma instructed him to compose the Ramayana. The chapter summarizes the six kandas (Balakanda, Ayodhyakanda, Aranyakanda, Kishkindhakanda, Sundarakanda, Yuddhakanda, Uttarakanda) and key events including Rama's birth, exile, Sita's abduction, Hanuman's exploits, the war with Ravana, and Rama's return. It also mentions the reunion of Rama with his sons Lava and Kusha.

beats.title: The Ashvamedha Yajna and the Liberation of the Horse
beats.summary: This chapter describes the continuation of Sri Rama's Ashvamedha yajna. Sita arrives at the yajna site, is welcomed by the queens and sages. Rama performs the yajna with Vasistha's guidance, honors the brahmins and sages, and then proceeds to consecrate the horse. The horse is bathed in sanctified water from the Sarayu, and upon being touched by Rama, it transforms into a divine being. This being reveals his past life as a brahmin who was cursed by Durvasa for hypocrisy, but was blessed to become the horse and attain liberation through Rama's touch. He then departs to the eternal abode.

beats.title: The Grand Sacrifice of Shri Rama and the Glory of Vrindavana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the completion of a grand sacrifice (yajna) by Lord Shri Ramachandra, where deities including Indra, Narayana, Mahadeva, Brahma, Varuna, and Kubera partake of the offerings. After the sacrifice, Lord Rama performs the avabhrita (consecratory bath) in the Sarayu river with Sita, adorned with divine ornaments. The chapter also extols the glory of Vrindavana and Shri Krishna, and concludes with the benefits of hearing this narrative, such as liberation from sins and attainment of the supreme abode.

beats.title: The Glories of Vrindavan and the Form of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of Vrindavan and the divine form of Lord Krishna. It emphasizes that Vrindavan is a transcendental realm free from sorrow, death, anger, envy, and ego. It is the eternal abode of Krishna, full of bliss and love. The chapter then details Krishna's beautiful form, including his complexion, ornaments, and posture, and explains that meditating on this form leads to liberation.

beats.title: Description of Radha-Krishna and Their Associates
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the divine forms and pastimes of Radha and Krishna, their associates, and the glories of devotion. It includes a description of Krishna's beauty, the eight principal sakhis, the gopas, and a visit by Narada to Nanda's house where he praises the child Krishna.

beats.title: Narada Discovers Radha in Vraja
beats.summary: Narada visits Vraja, meets the gopa Bhanu and his children, recognizes the divine nature of Bhanu's daughter as Radha, and praises her as the supreme goddess. He then witnesses her transformation and the arrival of other gopis, who reveal her identity as Krishna's beloved.

beats.title: The Glory of Mathura and Krishna's Divine Form
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Mathura and Vrindavan, the divine nature of a girl (likely Radha), and the supreme form of Lord Krishna. It includes a dialogue between Narada and a cowherd, and later between King Ambarisha and Sage Vedavyasa, where Vedavyasa recounts his vision of Krishna's eternal form and the sacredness of Mathura. The chapter emphasizes that Mathura grants liberation, and that devotion to Krishna and his associates is paramount.

beats.title: Krishna's Return and the Path of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter describes Krishna's return to Vrindavan and Dwarka, his liberation of the residents, and then shifts to a discourse by Shiva to Parvati on the twelve purifications (dvadasha shuddhi) and the five types of worship (abhigamana, upadana, yoga, svadhyaya, ijya). It also details the iconography of Vishnu's various forms based on the arrangement of weapons in his four hands, and the characteristics of Shaligram stones representing different deities.

beats.title: The Glory of Shalagrama and Gomati Chakra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the characteristics and forms of the Shalagrama stone, which is considered a manifestation of Lord Vishnu. It details the different types of Shalagrama stones associated with various deities such as Ananta, Damodara, Hayagriva, Matsya, Rama, and others, based on their markings, colors, and shapes. The chapter also explains the significance of the Gomati Chakra (Dwarka stone), the benefits of worshiping these stones, the rules for their examination, and the offenses to be avoided in Vishnu worship. It concludes with a list of forbidden foods for Vaishnavas and the importance of chanting the Lord's name.

beats.title: The Glory of Tulsi and Vishnu Worship in Kali Yuga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of Tulsi (basil) in worship, the power of chanting the names of Vishnu/Krishna, the description of Vishnu's foot-prints, and the monthly rituals for Vishnu worship, especially in Kali Yuga.

beats.title: The Mantra-chintamani and Rituals for Krishna Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various rituals and practices for worshiping Lord Krishna, including the Dolotsava (swing festival) in spring, the offering of fragrant substances, and the importance of devotion. It also narrates the dialogue between Shiva and Narada about the 'Mantra-chintamani' (a ten-syllable mantra) and its universal accessibility to all devotees regardless of caste or background.

beats.title: The Glory of Radha-Krishna and the Process of Initiation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the mantra of Shri Krishna and Radha, their meditation, the supremacy of Radha as the supreme goddess, the hierarchy of sacred places culminating in Vrindavan, the qualifications of a guru and disciple, and the process of initiation (diksha) for liberation.

beats.title: Instructions on Surrender and Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the duties of a disciple towards the guru, the process of initiation (diksha), and the external and internal practices of a surrendered devotee (sharanagata). It emphasizes devotion to Radha and Krishna, the importance of the guru, and the ultimate goal of attaining servitude to the divine couple. The chapter also includes a narrative where the sage Narada receives instructions from Lord Narayana.

beats.title: Krishna's Revelation to Shiva and the Glory of Vrindavan
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Padma Purana, Lord Krishna reveals his divine form to Shiva (Rudra) in Vrindavan, explaining the nature of his eternal abode, the supremacy of Radha, and the path of surrender and devotion. The narrative includes the dialogue between Krishna and Shiva, later recounted by Narada to King Ambarisha, and finally by Suta to the sages.

beats.title: Narada Instructs Ambarisha on Devotion
beats.summary: King Ambarisha welcomes Sage Narada and asks how to worship the supreme Lord Narayana, who is beyond thought and form. Narada explains the importance of devotion, the power of holy company, and describes the vows and practices for pleasing Vishnu.

beats.title: Discourse on Devotion and Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of devotion to Vishnu, the methods of worship including meditation on both saguna and nirguna forms, the significance of serving brahmins, and the role of women in serving their husbands as a form of worship. It also describes the eight flowers of worship (non-violence, sense-control, compassion, forgiveness, etc.) and the various places where Vishnu can be worshipped.

beats.title: The Two Types of Meditation and the Fivefold Bhakti
beats.summary: This chapter describes the two types of meditation on Vishnu: nirguna (formless) and saguna (with form). It details the physical attributes of Vishnu's saguna form, then explains the five types of bhakti (devotion): manasi, vaciki, kayiki, laukiki, and vaidiki, along with adhyatmiki (yogic) bhakti. The chapter emphasizes that devotion to Vishnu destroys all sins and leads to liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Vaishakha and Ganga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the glory of the month of Vaishakha (Madhava masa), especially the bath in the Ganga on the seventh day of the bright fortnight. It describes the greatness of Ganga, the importance of Vaishakha snana, and the superiority of this month over others. The dialogue is between Narada and King Ambarisha, where Narada explains the virtues of Vaishakha and the proper rituals.

beats.title: The Glory of Vaishakha Snana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Vaishakha month, emphasizing the importance of bathing, charity, and devotion to Lord Vishnu. It narrates the story of King Ambarisha and sage Narada discussing the merits of Vaishakha snana, the power of the name 'Narayana', and the liberation of five ghosts through the grace of the month.

beats.title: Redemption of Five Sinners
beats.summary: Five sinners (a brahmin-killer, a guru-killer, a man who committed incest with his mother, a thief, and a man who associated with sinners) approach the sage Munisharma seeking redemption. He takes them to the Narmada river, where they bathe and hear the Papapraharanam stotra, which cleanses their sins. The chapter extols the virtues of Vaishakha month, Ganga, Narmada, and devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: Complete (part 278)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana contains a hymn (stotra) for the removal of sins, attributed to King Ambarisha. It describes the power of chanting the names of Vishnu (Hrishikesha, Pundarikaksha, Madhava, Vasudeva) to destroy all sins committed by body, mind, and speech. It also details the rituals for the month of Vaishakha, including bathing, tarpaṇa, and worship of Madhava (Vishnu), with specific mantras and procedures.

beats.title: Rituals of Vaishakha Month
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the rituals and practices for the month of Vaishakha, including tarpaṇa (water offerings) to sages, ancestors, and deities, as well as the worship of Lord Vishnu (Madhusudana). It details the procedures for bathing, charity, fasting, and the types of worship (Vedic, Tantric, mixed). The importance of devotion and proper conduct is emphasized.

beats.title: Worship of Vishnu and Karmic Consequences
beats.summary: This chapter describes the detailed procedure for worshiping Lord Vishnu, including meditation, offerings, homa, and temple construction. It emphasizes the importance of devotion, charity, and righteous conduct. The dialogue between Yama and a Brahmana explains the karmic consequences leading to heaven or hell, highlighting the power of Vishnu's name and the virtues that lead to liberation.

beats.title: Paths to Heaven and Glories of Vaishakha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various paths to heaven, including chanting Vishnu's name, observing Ekadashi, bathing in sacred rivers, residing in holy places like Dwaraka and Badrikashram, performing shraddha, and practicing charity and truthfulness. It also emphasizes the importance of Tulsi leaves in worship and the glory of the Vaishakha month.

beats.title: The Liberation of Three Ghosts through Vaishakha Rites
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance of bathing, charity, and rituals during the month of Vaishakha, especially on the full moon day. It narrates a story of a Brahmin named Dhanarama who encounters three ghosts (pretas) in a forest. The ghosts explain their past sins: one was ungrateful, another neglected worship of deities, and the third failed to perform Vaishakha rituals. Dhanarama learns that one ghost is his own father. The father instructs him to perform Vaishakha rites to liberate them. Dhanarama follows the instructions, and the ghosts are freed.

beats.title: The Glory of Vaishakha and the Story of King Mahiratha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana (Patala Khanda) describes the glory of the Vaishakha month, emphasizing the importance of bathing, charity, and worship of Vishnu during this period. It includes the story of King Mahiratha, who was addicted to sensual pleasures and was advised by his priest Kasyapa to follow dharma. The chapter also contains teachings on the transient nature of life, the importance of dharma, and the power of the Vaishakha vow to destroy sins.

beats.title: The Story of King Mahiratha and the Vaisakha Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter concludes the story of King Mahiratha and the dialogue between Yama and a Brahmin. It emphasizes controlling desire and anger, the importance of Dharma, and the purifying power of the Vaisakha month. King Mahiratha, after receiving advice from his priest Kashyapa, performs the Vaisakha rites and attains liberation. Vishnu's messengers take him to Vaikuntha, bypassing hell.

beats.title: King Mahiratha's Journey Through Hell and Redemption
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various hells (narakas) where sinners suffer, and highlights the power of the Vaishakha month's morning bath and compassion. King Mahiratha, brought to hell by Vishnu's messengers, learns that his Vaishakha observances and compassion have purified him, and he is taken to Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: The Glory of Vaishakha and Meditation on Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the month of Vaishakha (Madhava month). King Mahiratha, after hearing from Vishnudutas, transfers his merit from a single day's Vaishakha observances to a group of sinners, freeing them from hell. The chapter also includes a detailed meditation on Lord Krishna in Vrindavana, as taught by Narada to Gautama.

beats.title: The Divine Form of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the divine form of Lord Krishna, adorned with various ornaments and yellow garments, playing the flute amidst cows and gopas. The scene includes deities, sages, and celestial beings surrounding him in devotion.

beats.title: The Glory of Badrikashrama and the Lineage of Sagara
beats.summary: This chapter begins with a meditation on Lord Krishna and the sage Narada, followed by a dialogue between Narada and Lord Shiva (Mahadeva) at Badrikashrama. Shiva describes the glory of Badrikashrama, the two sages Nara-Narayana, and the sacred river Ganga. He then narrates the story of King Haridra and his lineage, including King Sagara, who performed an Ashvamedha sacrifice and dug the ocean, leading to the encounter with Sage Kapila.

beats.title: The Descent of the Ganges and Its Glorification
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of the Ganges river, its origin from Vishnu's feet, and its descent to earth through the efforts of King Bhagiratha. It also narrates the story of King Sagar's sons and the birth of Anshuman, Dilipa, and Bhagiratha. The chapter includes praises of Ganga, Yamuna, and other sacred rivers, and emphasizes the purifying power of Ganga's water and the importance of bathing in her.

beats.title: Glorification of Ganga and Prayaga
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to the river Ganga (Ganges) and the holy place Prayaga (confluence of Ganga, Yamuna, and Saraswati). It extols the purifying power of Ganga, who is described as the destroyer of sins and the boat to cross the ocean of suffering. The chapter also glorifies Prayaga as the king of holy places, where even gods like Brahma, Vishnu, and Indra come to bathe. The text encourages the mind not to fear hell, as the grace of Ganga and Prayaga can liberate even the greatest sinners.

beats.title: Glorification of Holy Places and Deities
beats.summary: This chapter glorifies the holy places Prayag, Gaya, and Kashi, and the deities associated with them. It describes the benefits of bathing in Manikarnika, performing shraddha at Gaya, and reciting the stotra of the deities. The text also includes a prayer to Gadadhara (Vishnu) and praises Tulsi, Shaligram, and Prayag.

beats.title: Glories of Tulsi and Prayag
beats.summary: This chapter extols the virtues of Tulsi (holy basil) wood in funeral pyres, worship, and daily practices, and describes the glory of Prayag (confluence of Ganga, Yamuna, and Saraswati) and the Akshayavat tree. It emphasizes that using Tulsi wood in cremation liberates the soul, and that worship with Tulsi grants immense merit. Prayag is declared the best of tirthas, and bathing there, especially in Magha, removes sins and grants liberation.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 6)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Prayaga Tirtha and the tri-ratri tulasi vrata (three-night vow involving tulasi). It includes the procedure for worshiping Vishnu and Lakshmi, offerings, mantras, and the benefits of the vow.

beats.title: The Glorification of Charity and Truth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the merits of various charitable acts and virtues, including food donation, water donation, pond construction, tree planting, and truthfulness. It emphasizes the supreme importance of truth and austerity (tapas) for attaining liberation and heavenly realms.

beats.title: The Story of King Vaidida and the Brahmin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the power of tapasya (austerity), the importance of studying and teaching the Vedas and Puranas, the merit of donating to worthy recipients, and the glory of applying gopichandana tilaka. It also narrates a story about King Vaidida who built a Vishnu temple and sponsored Purana recitations, leading to his and a Brahmin's attainment of divine rewards.

beats.title: The Vow of the Annual Lamp
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Samvatsaradipa-vrata (the vow of the annual lamp), a ritual for attaining liberation and Vishnu's abode. It details the procedure: waking at Brahma-muhurta, bathing, worshiping Vishnu with Lakshmi, offering a lamp that burns continuously for a year, and concluding with a ceremony involving brahmins. The chapter emphasizes the power of this vow to destroy sins and grant merit equal to many donations.

beats.title: The Glory of Dipadana and Jayanti Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the benefits of the Dipadana (lamp offering) vow and the Jayanti (Janmashtami) vow. It explains how offering lamps to Vishnu brings various rewards such as wealth, sons, liberation from sins, and ultimately Vishnu's abode. The story of King Harishchandra (in a previous birth as a poor merchant) is narrated to illustrate the power of the Jayanti vow, which involves worshipping Krishna and Yashoda on the Ashtami of Krishna Paksha with Rohini nakshatra.

beats.title: The Glorification of Charity
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the importance of various donations, especially land, cows, gold, and water bodies. It describes the merits of donating land (bhūmidāna), the consequences of stealing land, and the characteristics of a worthy recipient. The dialogue is between Indra and his guru Bṛhaspati, who explains the eternal benefits of charity and the severe punishments for misappropriating a Brahmin's property.

beats.title: King Dasharatha's Encounter with Shani and the Trispara Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Dasharatha's encounter with Shani (Saturn). When Shani's transit threatens the world, Dasharatha confronts him, offers a hymn of praise, and receives a boon that Shani will not harm those who worship him properly. The chapter also introduces the Trispara vrata, a vow that destroys sins and grants liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Trispara Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Trispara vrata (a fast observed when the eleventh day of the lunar fortnight coincides with the twelfth day and the last part of the night). It is said to be superior to thousands of sacrifices and holy baths, granting liberation even to those attached to worldly pleasures. The dialogue between Narada and Shiva, and between Ganga and Madhava (Krishna) on the banks of the Prachi Sarasvati, explains the vrata's glory and procedure. The chapter also discusses the Pakshavardhini ekadashi and its benefits.

beats.title: The Merits of Ekadashi and Night Vigil
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and merits of observing the Ekadashi fast, especially the Pakshavardhini Ekadashi, and the importance of night vigil (jagaran) in honor of Lord Vishnu. It details the procedure of worship, offering arghya, naivedya, and the significance of chanting names, dancing, and music. It warns against faults in observance and emphasizes the unity of Vishnu and Shiva.

beats.title: Glory of Ekadashi and Dwadashi Vratas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Ekadashi and Dwadashi vratas, especially the Jagran (vigil) and the special Ekadashis like Jaya, Vijaya, Jayanti, and Papanashini. It explains the benefits of observing these fasts, the importance of Dwadashi, and the procedure for the vrata. It also includes a dialogue between Yudhishthira and Lord Krishna about the Naktavrata and Ekabhukta vrata.

beats.title: The Glory of Ekadashi and the Defeat of Mura
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Ekadashi fasting, its superiority over other rituals, and narrates the story of the demon Mura who defeated the gods, leading them to seek refuge in Vishnu. Vishnu then goes to Badrikashrama to fight the demon.

beats.title: The Glory of Ekadashi: The Story of Mura and King Vaikhanasa
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the demon Mura, who is killed by a goddess who emerges from Lord Vishnu's body. The goddess is identified as Ekadashi. Lord Vishnu grants her a boon that those who fast on her day will attain liberation. The chapter then describes the glory of Ekadashi, especially the Moksha Ekadashi in the Margashirsha month, and tells the story of King Vaikhanasa who performed the Moksha Ekadashi vow to rescue his ancestors from hell. The chapter also introduces the Saphala and Putrada Ekadashis in the Pausha month.

beats.title: Glories of Saphala and Putrada Ekadashis
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glories of two Ekadashis: Saphala Ekadashi and Putrada Ekadashi. It narrates the story of King Mahishmat's sinful son Dambhaka who, through accidental observance of Saphala Ekadashi, attained liberation. It also tells the story of a childless king who, by observing Putrada Ekadashi, obtained a son.

beats.title: The Glories of Shattila and Jaya Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the importance of the Putrada vrata and two Ekadashis in the month of Magha: Shattila and Jaya. It narrates the story of the Gandharvas Malyavan and Pushpavanti who were cursed to become Pisachas but were liberated by observing the Jaya Ekadashi vrata.

beats.title: The Glory of Vijayā and Āmalakī Ekādaśī
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and significance of the Vijayā Ekādaśī and Āmalakī Ekādaśī fasts. It narrates how Lord Rāma, while searching for Sītā, was advised by sage Bakadālbhya to observe the Vijayā Ekādaśī to gain victory over Rāvaṇa. The chapter also recounts the origin of the Āmalakī tree from a drop of Viṣṇu's saliva and its sacred nature, as told by Vasiṣṭha to King Māndhātā.

beats.title: The Glory of Amalaki Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Amalaki Ekadashi vrata (fast) observed in the Phalguna month, as narrated by Lord Vishnu to the Kumaras and later by Vasishtha to a king, and by Krishna to Yudhishthira. It includes the story of the sage Medhavi being tempted by the apsara Manjughosha, and the glory of the vrata.

beats.title: The Power of Papamocani and Kamada Ekadashis
beats.summary: This chapter narrates two stories about the power of specific Ekadashi fasts. First, the sage Medhavi is seduced by the apsara Manjugosha and loses his penance for 57 years; he curses her to become a pisacha, but she is liberated by observing the Papamocani Ekadashi. Second, the gandharva Lalita (also called Kinkita) is cursed by King Pundarika to become a rakshasa for being distracted by his wife during a performance; his wife Lalita learns from a sage that observing the Kamada Ekadashi and transferring its merit can free him. The chapter also introduces Varuthini Ekadashi.

beats.title: Glories of Varuthini, Mohini, and Apara Ekadashis
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of Varuthini and Mohini Ekadashis in the month of Vaishakha, and Apara Ekadashi in Jyeshtha. It includes stories of a Gandharva couple liberated by Varuthini Ekadashi, and a sinful son Dhrishtabuddhi who attained liberation by observing Mohini Ekadashi. The chapter also discusses the hierarchy of donations and rules for Ekadashi observance.

beats.title: The Glory of Nirjala Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glories of the Nirjala Ekadashi (also called Pandava Ekadashi) occurring in the bright fortnight of Jyeshtha month. It narrates the conversation between Bhima and sage Vyasa, where Bhima expresses his inability to fast on all Ekadashis due to his insatiable hunger. Vyasa advises him to observe the Nirjala Ekadashi, which involves complete fasting even from water, and explains its immense merits, including liberation from sins, attainment of Vishnu's abode, and benefits for ancestors. The chapter also details the rituals, donations, and the importance of listening to its glory.

beats.title: Glories of Yogini and Kamika Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glories of the Ekadashi fasts, particularly the Yogini Ekadashi in the dark fortnight of Ashadha and the Kamika Ekadashi in the dark fortnight of Shravana. It includes the story of the yaksha Hemamali who was cursed by Kubera for neglecting Shiva's worship, then instructed by sage Markandeya to observe the Yogini Ekadashi, which freed him from leprosy. The chapter also discusses rules for Ekadashi observance and the importance of Chaturmasya vows.

beats.title: Glories of Kamika, Putrada, and Aja Ekadashis
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Kamika Ekadashi and Putrada Ekadashi, including the story of King Mahijit who obtained a son through the observance of Putrada Ekadashi. It also introduces the sage Lomasha and the story of King Harishchandra in relation to Aja Ekadashi.

beats.title: The Glory of Padya Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Ekadashi fast, particularly the 'Padya' Ekadashi in the bright fortnight of Bhadrapada. It includes stories of King Harishchandra's suffering and redemption through the fast, and King Mandhata's drought relief by observing the same fast. The chapter also provides the ritual details and a mantra for the fast.

beats.title: Glories of Indira and Papankusha Ekadashis
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glories of two Ekadashis in the month of Ashvin: Indira Ekadashi (Krishna Paksha) and Papankusha Ekadashi (Shukla Paksha). It narrates the story of King Indrasena who, advised by Narada, observes Indira Ekadashi to liberate his father from a degraded birth. The chapter also extols the benefits of observing Papankusha Ekadashi, including liberation from sins and attainment of Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: The Glory of Rama Ekadashi and Prabodhini Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Muchukunda, his daughter Chandrabhaga, and her husband Shobhana, who observes the Rama Ekadashi vow and attains a divine city on Mandarachala. The chapter also describes the glory of Prabodhini Ekadashi, as told by Brahma to Narada and by Krishna to Yudhishthira.

beats.title: The Story of the Sinful Brahmin and Kamala Ekadashi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the virtues of the Ekadashi fast, especially the Prabodhini Ekadashi in Kartik month, and the Kamala Ekadashi in the Purushottam month. It describes the benefits of listening to and reciting Vishnu's stories, offering Tulsi leaves, and performing rituals. A story is told of a sinful Brahmin who, after hearing the Kamala Ekadashi story, is visited by Goddess Lakshmi and granted a boon.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 31)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance and rituals of Ekadashi and Chaturmasya vratas. It emphasizes fasting on Ekadashi, especially Kamada Ekadashi, and the rules for Chaturmasya, including abstaining from certain foods and activities. The chapter also discusses the benefits of these observances, such as liberation and attaining Vishnu's abode.

beats.title: Merits of Chaturmasya Observances
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the merits and rules of various observances during the four-month period (Chaturmasya), including dietary restrictions, fasting, charity, and worship of Vishnu. It emphasizes the importance of eating on palasha leaves, avoiding certain foods, and performing acts like bathing with amla, giving cows, and reciting Vedas. The chapter also warns against sins like stealing from Brahmins and improper conduct.

beats.title: Chaturmasya Vrata and Vaitarani Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the rules and benefits of the Chaturmasya vrata (four-month vow), including its observance, the importance of donations to Brahmins, and the udyapana (concluding ceremony). It also describes the Vaitarani vrata, the worship of Yama, and the glory of Gopichandana. The narrative includes a dialogue between Narada and Mahadeva, and a story about Yama's messengers and a sage named Mudgara.

beats.title: Ritual Worship of Vishnu and Yama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of bathing, worship of Vishnu and Yama (Dharmaraja), including the installation of a kalasha, making a mandala, offering arghya to Vaitarani river, and reciting various mantras. It also includes the worship of the cow and the significance of Gopichandana.

beats.title: The Merchant Lost in the Desert
beats.summary: This chapter describes the worship of cows, especially the Nandini cow, with mantras and rituals. It extols the virtues of Gopichandana (sacred clay) and Tulsi wood, and explains the characteristics and glory of Vaishnavas. It also details the Shravana-dvadashi vrata (fast on the twelfth lunar day when the moon is in Shravana nakshatra) and narrates a story of a merchant lost in a desert who encounters ghosts.

beats.title: The Merchant and the Ghost
beats.summary: A merchant gets lost in a forest and meets a ghost (preta) who offers him food. The ghost explains his past life as a miserly merchant who once performed a charitable act on Shravana-dwadashi, which now provides him with inexhaustible food. The ghost asks the merchant to perform shraddha for him and his companions at Gaya. The merchant does so, and the ghost attains liberation. The chapter then shifts to a dialogue between Suta and sages about the glory of Vishnu's names, emphasizing the power of nama-kirtana.

beats.title: The Power of Vishnu's Names and Narada's Quest
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the power of chanting the names of Vishnu, especially in Kali Yuga. It describes how mere utterance of names like Krishna, Rama, and the ten avatars (Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, Krishna, Buddha, Kalki) can purify even great sinners. Narada, after learning from Brahma, approaches Shiva on Mount Kailasa to receive the Vishnu Sahasranama (thousand names). Shiva, moved by devotion, teaches Narada the sacred names. Later, Narada visits Naimisharanya where sages honor him and request teachings on liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of Vishnu Sahasranama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance of devotion to Vishnu, the recitation of the Vishnu Sahasranama, and the associated rituals including nyasa and dhyana. It begins with Parvati asking Shiva about the means to attain liberation, and Shiva explains that in the Satya Yuga, people attained liberation through chanting Vishnu's names. He then narrates the glory of the Vishnu Sahasranama and provides the mantra, nyasa, and dhyana procedures.

beats.title: Commentary on Vishnu Sahasranama
beats.summary: This chapter is a commentary on the Vishnu Sahasranama, explaining the meanings of various names of Vishnu. It describes the supreme being as beyond all attributes, the cause of all, and the ultimate reality. The text lists and interprets names such as Nirguna, Niskala, Ananta, etc., emphasizing Vishnu's transcendence and immanence.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 40)
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of names and attributes of Vishnu, describing his various forms, powers, and relationships with other deities. It includes epithets such as Aniruddha, Hrishikesha, and many others, emphasizing his supremacy and role as creator, preserver, and destroyer.

beats.title: Vishnu Sahasranama Stotra (Part)
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Vishnu, listing his numerous names and attributes, each compared to crores of various entities like Durga, oceans, Brahmas, etc. It extols his supreme nature, power, and benevolence.

beats.title: Vishnu Sahasranama: Names and Glories
beats.summary: This chapter is a continuation of the Vishnu Sahasranama (thousand names of Vishnu) with commentary. It lists and explains various names of Vishnu, describing his attributes, avatars, and deeds. The text includes cross-references to avatars like Matsya, Kurma, Varaha, Narasimha, and others, and highlights Vishnu's role as the supreme deity.

beats.title: Hymn of Narasimha's Glories
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Lord Narasimha, describing his various names, attributes, and deeds. It highlights his role as the protector of his devotee Prahlada, the destroyer of the demon Hiranyakashipu, and the supreme lord who vanquishes all evils and grants boons to his devotees.

beats.title: Vishnu Sahasranama: Names and Incarnations
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana lists and describes various names and epithets of Vishnu, including his incarnations such as Vamana, Parashurama, Rama, and others, along with their attributes and deeds.

beats.title: Glorification of Lord Rama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana presents a series of epithets and descriptions of Lord Rama, highlighting his divine attributes, heroic deeds, and role as an avatar of Vishnu. It recounts key events from the Ramayana, including his exile, battles with demons, and restoration of dharma.

beats.title: Description of Divine Beings and Avatars
beats.summary: This chapter describes various divine beings and their attributes, focusing on the avatars and manifestations of Vishnu, including Rama, Lakshmana, Bharata, Shatrughna, and others. It also mentions Kapila, Shiva, and other deities, highlighting their roles and characteristics.

beats.title: The Glories of Lord Krishna and Balarama
beats.summary: This chapter lists numerous names and epithets of Lord Krishna and Balarama, describing their divine exploits and attributes. It includes references to their avatars, such as Balarama as an incarnation of Shesha, and Krishna's various deeds like killing demons, lifting Govardhana, and protecting devotees.

beats.title: Names and Deeds of Vishnu/Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana lists various names and epithets of Vishnu/Krishna, describing his deeds such as granting boons, protecting devotees, slaying demons, and his incarnations including Buddha and Kalki.

beats.title: Vishnu Sahasranama or Enumeration of Divine Manifestations
beats.summary: This chapter lists various manifestations and attributes of the divine, including deities, sages, kings, and concepts, as part of a hymn or enumeration. It includes names like Vishnu, Indra, Prahlada, and others, along with their descriptions and roles.

beats.title: The Glorification of Vishnu Sahasranama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the greatness of the Vishnu Sahasranama (the thousand names of Vishnu). It describes the benefits of reciting, chanting, or hearing these names, including removal of sins, fulfillment of desires, liberation, and protection from calamities. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Shiva and Parvati, where Shiva emphasizes the supremacy of Vishnu and the power of His names, especially the name 'Rama'. Instructions are given on who should receive this stotra and how it should be practiced.

beats.title: Glory of Grihastha Dharma and Gandaki River
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the virtues of the householder (grihastha) dharma, emphasizing its superiority among ashramas and the importance of charity and hospitality. It then describes the glory of the Gandaki River, its sacredness, and the presence of Vishnu's avatars there. Finally, it recounts the Urdhvadehika stotra, a hymn originally recited by Brahma to Rama after Ravana's defeat, which absolves even great sinners.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 52)
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn (stotra) praising Lord Vishnu (as Rama) as the supreme being, describing his cosmic form, avatars (Varaha, Vamana, Narasimha, Hayagriva), and his role as the creator, sustainer, and destroyer. It includes a dialogue between Shiva and Parvati about the best vow, and a story of a Brahmin named Devarama who observes a vow on the fifth day (Panchami).

beats.title: The Rishi Panchami Vrata and the Liberation of the Brahmin's Parents
beats.summary: This chapter describes the story of a Brahmin who discovers that his parents have been reborn as a dog and a bull due to past sins related to neglecting the Rishi Panchami vrata. The sage Vasishta explains the origin and proper observance of the Rishi Panchami vrata, which can liberate them. The chapter also includes instructions for performing the vrata and a mantra for offering arghya to the seven sages.

beats.title: Rishi Panchami Vrata and Nyasa Vidhi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Rishi Panchami vrata, its benefits, and a detailed nyasa (ritual placement) of various forms of Vishnu on the body, along with a hymn (Apamārjana stotra) for curing diseases and removing obstacles. It also includes a dialogue between Shiva and Parvati.

beats.title: The Apamārjana Stotra: A Hymn for Healing and Protection
beats.summary: This chapter presents the Apamārjana Stotra, a hymn dedicated to Lord Viṣṇu, which is used for healing diseases, removing poisons, pacifying malevolent spirits and planetary afflictions, and destroying sins. It describes the power of the stotra and its proper usage, emphasizing selfless application by devotees. The stotra is said to have been transmitted from Brahmā to Pulastya, then to Dālbhya, and spread throughout the three worlds.

beats.title: The Story of Pundarika
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of the sage Pundarika, a devoted Brahmin who renounces worldly attachments and practices severe austerities at the Devahrada tirtha on the Sarasvati river. He is visited by Narada, who praises Vishnu's glory and instructs him on the highest duty. The chapter also includes a dialogue between Yudhishthira and Bhishma about the best path to liberation, with Bhishma recounting Pundarika's story.

beats.title: Discourse on Supreme Knowledge
beats.summary: This chapter discusses various philosophical views about the nature of the world and the self, ultimately asserting the supremacy of Lord Vishnu (Narayana) as the ultimate reality. It includes a dialogue between Narada and Pundarika, where Narada recounts teachings from Brahma about the supreme knowledge and yoga, emphasizing that Vishnu is the creator, preserver, and destroyer of the universe, and that all beings and elements are pervaded by him.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 58)
beats.summary: This chapter emphasizes the supremacy of Lord Narayana as the father, mother, and lord of all worlds. It extols the power of the mantra 'Om Namo Narayanaya' and the importance of devotion over external signs. It narrates the stories of King Kshatrabandhu, King Ambarisha, King Mitrasana, Prahlada, and King Bharata, who attained liberation through devotion to Vishnu. The main story is about the devotee Pundarika, who, through intense devotion and chanting of the mantra, attained the vision of Lord Vishnu and was blessed. The chapter concludes with Pundarika's hymn of praise to the Lord.

beats.title: The Glory of Devotion and the Ganges
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of Pundarika, a devotee who is blessed by Lord Vishnu. Vishnu appears as Vamana (the dwarf avatar) and grants Pundarika a boon, taking him to Vaikuntha. The chapter also extols the glory of the Ganges river, the characteristics of Vaishnavas, and the importance of devotion to Vishnu. It includes a dialogue between Bhishma and Yudhishthira, and a conversation between Shiva and Parvati about the Ganges and Vaishnava dharma.

beats.title: The Glories of Dolotsava and Jalashayana
