beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the importance of worshiping Lord Vishnu, especially in the months of Chaitra and Vaishakha. It details the rituals for the Dolotsava (swing festival) and Jalashayana (water sleeping) ceremonies, emphasizing the benefits of devotion and the proper methods of worship, including the use of mantras, offerings, and the construction of idols.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 61)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the rituals and merits of placing Lord Vishnu in water during the months of Jyeshtha and Ashadha, the procedure for Pavitraropana (sacred thread offering) to Vishnu and other deities, and the associated mantras and benefits.

beats.title: The Glory of Pavitraropana and Kartik-vrata
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ritual of Pavitraropana (offering sacred thread) to Lord Vishnu, including mantras, offerings, and the importance of devotion. It also details the specific flowers to be used for worship in each month of the Hindu calendar, as narrated by Shiva to Parvati. Additionally, it recounts the story of Gunavati, who through the Kartik-vrata became a queen of Krishna, illustrating the power of the vow.

beats.title: The Glorification of Kartik Vrata and the Story of Gunavati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of Gunavati, a virtuous woman who performed the Kartik vrata (austerities during the month of Kartik) and was rewarded with liberation and union with Lord Vishnu. It also includes the tale of the demon Shankhasura's defeat by Vishnu and the glorification of the Kartik month.

beats.title: The Greatness of Kartik and Magh Months and the Matsya Avatar
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the greatness of Kartik and Magh months, the story of Matsya avatar rescuing Vedas from Shankhasura, the establishment of Prayag as Tirtharaja, and the rules for Kartik vrata including proper hygiene and rituals.

beats.title: Kartik Month Rituals and the Glory of Devotees
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana details the rules and procedures for performing Kartik month rituals, including toothbrush usage, bathing, and worship of Vishnu and Shiva. It specifies forbidden flowers and trees for worship, emphasizes the importance of devotion over ritual perfection, and includes a dialogue between Narada and Vishnu where Vishnu states he resides where his devotees sing his praises.

beats.title: The Kartik Vow: Rules and Udyapana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rules and procedures for the Kartik vow (vrata), including daily practices, prohibitions, and the concluding ceremony (udyapana). It emphasizes the importance of bathing, worship of Vishnu and Tulsi, charity, and listening to sacred stories. The vow is said to grant liberation and protection from Yama's servants.

beats.title: The Kartik Vrata and the Redemption of Kalaha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Kartik vrata (vow) and its udyapana (concluding ceremony), emphasizing the importance of feeding Brahmins, donating cows and gold, and worshipping Vishnu with tulsi and amla. It narrates the story of a Brahmin named Dharmadatta who encounters a rakshasi (demoness) named Kalaha, who was a Brahmin woman in her past life but became a demoness due to her bad conduct. Through the power of the Kartik vrata and the Brahmin's sprinkling of tulsi-mixed water, she is purified and seeks liberation. The chapter extols the virtues of tulsi and amla trees, stating that they are dear to Vishnu and can destroy sins.

beats.title: The Redemption of Kalaha and the Glory of Kartik-vrata
beats.summary: A woman named Kalaha recounts her past sins and suffering as a pret (ghost) due to her misdeeds, including disobeying her husband, stealing sweets, and committing suicide. She is rescued by the sage Dharmadatta, who gives her half the merit of his Kartik-vrata and performs a ritual with tulsi water, freeing her from the pret body. She then attains a divine form and is taken to Vishnu's abode by his attendants Punyajna and Sujna. The attendants praise Dharmadatta and tell him he will also reach Vaikuntha. They then narrate the story of King Chola and Vishnudasa, highlighting the power of tulsi worship.

beats.title: The Contest of Devotion: King Chola and Vishnudas
beats.summary: King Chola and the brahmin Vishnudas both claim to be greater devotees of Vishnu. The king performs a Vaishnava yajna, while Vishnudas observes vows and daily worship. Vishnudas's food is stolen for seven days; he discovers a starving chandala, who is actually Lord Narayana in disguise. Vishnu reveals himself, embraces Vishnudas, and takes him to Vaikuntha. The king sees this and laments that Vishnudas attained liberation through devotion alone.

beats.title: The Power of Devotion and Shared Merit
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the importance of devotion (bhakti) over rituals for pleasing Lord Vishnu, narrates the story of King Chola who gave his kingdom to his nephew and jumped into a sacrificial fire, and describes how the sage Mudgal cursed his lineage. It also includes teachings on how one can share in others' merits and sins through association, and tells the story of the Brahmin Dhaneshvara who attained liberation through the merit of others.

beats.title: The Redemption of Dhaneshwara through Association with Devotees
beats.summary: This chapter describes the story of Dhaneshwara, a sinful Brahmin who, despite his wickedness, gains merit by associating with devotees performing Kartik-vrata. After death, he is taken to Yama's realm and thrown into Kumbhipaka hell, but the fire turns cold due to his accrued merit. Narada intervenes, explaining that his association with Kartik-vrata performers earned him a share of their merit. He is then shown various hells and finally taken to Yaksha-loka.

beats.title: The Glory of Kartik Month and Its Observances
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the Kartik month vow, its rules, and the means to observe it even in adverse conditions. It includes a dialogue between Suta and sages, and between Lord Shiva and Kartikeya, emphasizing the importance of Kartik month, the five observances (jagaran, snan, tulsi seva, udyapan, deepdan), and the equivalence of certain substitutes when one cannot perform the full vow.

beats.title: Merits of Kartika and Maasa Upavasa Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the merits of various holy practices, especially during the month of Kartika. It emphasizes the importance of bathing in the Gomati River, seeing Lord Krishna, observing Kartika vows, and donating to Brahmins. It also extols the virtues of the Sukara Kshetra (Varaha Kshetra) and the Maasa Upavasa Vrata (month-long fast). The chapter is narrated by Lord Shiva to Kartikeya and by Lord Krishna to Satyabhama.

beats.title: The Glories of Shalagrama Worship in Kartika
beats.summary: This chapter describes the rituals and merits of worshipping Lord Vishnu, especially through the Shalagrama shila, during the month of Kartika. It includes instructions for puja, the importance of Tulsi, lamp offerings, and the benefits of devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: Rituals of Kartik Month
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes rituals for Kartik month, including lamp offerings (dipadana) to Yamaraja on trayodashi, bathing with apamarga on chaturdashi, shraddha on amavasya, awakening Lakshmi, worship of Govardhana, and the significance of Yama-dwitiya (Bhai Dooj). It also covers Prabodhini Ekadashi and Bhishma-panchaka vrata.

beats.title: Bhishma-panchaka Vrata and the Glory of Devotion
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the Bhishma-panchaka vrata (five-day vow) observed during the Kartika month, including rituals, mantras, and the importance of devotion to Vishnu. It also discusses the three types of bhakti (sattvic, rajasic, tamasic) and the benefits of devotion to Govinda.

beats.title: The Glory of Shalagrama and Vishnu Bhakti
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of Shalagrama shila worship, the glory of Vishnu devotion, and the power of remembering Vishnu. It describes the benefits of worshipping Shalagrama, the characteristics of a true Vaishnava, and the supreme efficacy of bhakti yoga. The dialogue is between Parvati and Shiva, where Shiva explains the methods and fruits of devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: The Glory of Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the supremacy of devotion (bhakti) to Lord Vishnu, describing it as the only means to attain eternal bliss in Vaikuntha. It contrasts the impermanent fruits of Vedic rituals with the everlasting reward of bhakti, and uses analogies (swan separating milk from water, frog vs. bee, etc.) to illustrate that only the devoted can perceive the divine. The chapter also discusses the nature of the self (atman) and the all-pervading Vishnu, the process of creation from the unmanifest, and the importance of mental purity and inner detachment.

beats.title: Discourse on Devotion and Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the supremacy of devotion (bhakti) over mere ritualistic actions, the nature of karma and its fruits, the importance of pure intention in charity, the inevitability of experiencing the results of one's actions, and the glory of various tirthas (holy places). It emphasizes that devotion to Lord Vishnu or Krishna is the ultimate means to liberation, surpassing all other practices.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 80)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Vindhya mountain, the stepmotherly tirtha, and the Brahmamaya tirtha. It extols the virtues of taking the name of Lord Vishnu, which can absolve even the gravest sins. The chapter then focuses on the sacred rivers Vetravati (Betwa) and Sabarmati, narrating their origins and the purifying power of bathing in them. The story of King Vidaruna, a wicked ruler who was transformed after drinking water from the Vetravati, illustrates the river's redemptive power. The chapter also recounts how the sage Kashyapa obtained the Ganga from Lord Shiva and how the river is known by different names in different ages.

beats.title: The Greatness of Sabramati River and the Story of King Vaikartana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the greatness of the Sabramati River and its various subsidiary tirthas (sacred sites). It narrates the story of King Vaikartana, a sinful ruler who was purified by bathing in the Sabramati and attained liberation. The chapter also details the proper times for bathing and performing shraddha, and lists numerous tirthas that reside in the Sabramati by the command of Shiva.

beats.title: Glories of Sabarmati Tirthas and the Liberation of King Kukardama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin and glories of various tirthas (sacred sites) along the Sabarmati river, including Kapakunda, Vikirna, Shveta, Gana, Trivishtapa, and others. It narrates the story of King Kukardama who became a pret (ghost) due to his sinful life and was later liberated by the grace of his guru. The chapter also mentions the seven streams of Sabarmati and the benefits of bathing, donating, and performing shraddha at these tirthas.

beats.title: Liberation of the Pret Kakadarma and Glorification of Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the story of a pret (ghost) named Kakadarma from Pindarapura, who confesses his sins to a Brahmin named Kahoda and seeks liberation. Kahoda performs rituals at various tirthas (sacred sites) along the Sabramati river, including Hiranyasangama, Dharmatirtha, Madhuratirtha, Kapishvara, Ekadhara, Saptadhara, and Brahmavalli, leading to the liberation of the pret and eleven others. The chapter also narrates the origins and merits of these tirthas, involving deities like Vishnu, Shiva, Ganga, and figures like Rama, Hanuman, and the Pandavas.

beats.title: Glory of Tīrthas on the Sabarmatī
beats.summary: This chapter describes various holy tirthas (pilgrimage sites) along the Sabarmati river, including Khanda Tirtha, Sangameshvara, Rudramahālaya, Khadga Tirtha, Chitrāngavadana, Mālārka, Chandaneshvara, Jambūtīrtha, Indragrāma, and Dhavaleshvara. It narrates the story of Indra's sin of killing Namuchi and his purification at Indragrāma. The chapter emphasizes the glory of these tirthas, the importance of bathing, donating, and performing śrāddha there, and equates Rāma with Rudra.

beats.title: Glories of Sabarmati Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of various tirthas (sacred sites) along the Sabarmati river, especially the Dhwaleshwar, Balarka, Durvaseshwar, Khadgadhar, Dugdheshwar, and Chandradhar tirthas. It narrates the story of a bull that died in the mud of the Sabarmati and was reborn as a prince, who later installed a linga of Mahadeva. The chapter also explains the names of Shiva in different yugas and the benefits of bathing, donating, and worshipping at these tirthas.

beats.title: Tirthas along the Sabramati River
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes various tirthas (sacred sites) along the Sabramati river, their purifying powers, and the rituals to be performed there. It highlights the importance of bathing, donating, and worshipping deities like Vishnu, Shiva, and Surya. The text also recounts the story of a pigeon that gave its body to a guest and attained heaven, and mentions the establishment of the Pandurangini deity by Nakula.

beats.title: The Battle with Vṛtrāsura and the Disposal of Brahmahatyā
beats.summary: This chapter describes various holy tirthas (pilgrimage sites) and their merits, including Chandeśvara, Gaṇapati-tīrtha, and the confluence of Vārāṇasī. It narrates the story of Indra's battle with Vṛtrāsura, the emergence of Brahmahatyā (the sin of killing a brāhmaṇa), and how Brahmā distributed this sin among fire, trees, herbs, grass, and women during menstruation.

beats.title: Glory of Varaha Tirtha and Nrisimha Vrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Varaha Tirtha and the Sabhramati river, the story of Indra's liberation from brahmahatya, the origin of the Varaha Tirtha, and the Nrisimha Chaturdashi vrata. It includes a dialogue between Lord Nrisimha and Prahlada about the vrata's significance.

beats.title: The Narasimha Vrata and its Origin
beats.summary: This chapter describes the origin of the Narasimha vrata (fast) and its rituals. It narrates how Lord Narasimha appeared to the sage Harita and his wife Lilavati after their long penance, and granted them a boon. The chapter details the procedure for observing the Narasimha Chaturdashi vrata, including purification, worship, offerings, prayers, and the benefits of observing it. It also mentions the holy place associated with this story, the Sindhu river, and the town of Mulasthana.

beats.title: The Glory of the First Chapter of the Bhagavad Gita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the first chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. It begins with a dialogue between Parvati and Shiva, then moves to a conversation between Lakshmi and Vishnu, where Vishnu explains his nature and the significance of the Gita. The story of Sudama, a sinful man who attains liberation through the indirect hearing of the Gita's first chapter, is narrated. The chapter concludes with the liberation of a parrot, a prostitute, and a Brahmin through the same practice.

beats.title: The Glory of the Second Chapter of the Bhagavad Gita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the second chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. It narrates the story of a Brahmin named Devasarma who seeks peace and is directed to a goat-herder named Mitravan. Mitravan recounts how he attained wisdom through the second chapter of the Gita, which was inscribed on a stone slab in a temple. The chapter also includes a sub-story of a sage named Sukarma who received the second chapter from a guest and attained liberation.

beats.title: Liberation of the Damned through Gita Recitation
beats.summary: A son learns of his father's death and performs rituals including reciting Bhagavad Gita Chapter 3 to liberate his father and other suffering souls from hell. Vishnu's messengers command Yama to release the damned. Yama complies, and they all go to Vaikuntha where Yama praises Vishnu. The chapter ends with the beginning of the story of a yogi named Bharata in Varanasi.

beats.title: The Power of the Bhagavad Gita: Liberation of Cursed Souls
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a Brahmin who chanted the fourth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, leading to the liberation of two women who were cursed to become jujube trees. It also tells the story of Pingala, a Brahmin who was killed by his wife and later became a vulture, and the liberation of both through the power of the Gita's fifth chapter.

beats.title: The Purifying Power of the Gita's Chapters
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of two sinners who, after death, are saved by the accidental contact with water from the skull of a great sage who regularly recited the fifth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. Yama explains that the power of the Gita's fifth chapter purified them. Then, the chapter recounts the story of King Janashruti and the sage Raikva, highlighting the greatness of the sixth chapter of the Gita. The king, upon hearing swans praise Raikva's spiritual eminence, seeks him out, but Raikva initially rejects his gifts. The chapter emphasizes the purifying power of the Gita's chapters.

beats.title: Glories of the Seventh and Eighth Chapters of the Bhagavad Gita
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of the seventh and eighth chapters of the Bhagavad Gita. It narrates two stories: one about a Brahmin named Shankukarna who, after dying from a snakebite, becomes a serpent and is liberated by his son reciting the seventh chapter; and another about a sinful Brahmin named Bhavadama who becomes a brahmarakshasa and is liberated by hearing a half-verse from the eighth chapter. The chapter emphasizes the power of these Gita chapters to grant liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of the Ninth and Tenth Chapters of the Bhagavad Gita
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a Brahmin named Bhavadarma who, after hearing the greatness of the seventh and eighth chapters of the Bhagavad Gita, performs severe penance and attains liberation. It also includes the story of a Brahmin named Madhava in Mahishmati who, during a sacrifice, encounters a goat that speaks of its past life and the power of the ninth chapter of the Gita. The chapter highlights the glory of the ninth and tenth chapters of the Bhagavad Gita, emphasizing their power to grant liberation and overcome sins.

beats.title: The Swan and the Lotus-Nymph
beats.summary: A swan (Brahma's hamsa) visits a sage, recounts its fall and blackening due to flying over a lotus pond where a lotus-nymph (padmavati) was cursed by Durvasa. The nymph explains her past as a devoted wife who listened to the tenth chapter of the Gita (Vibhuti Yoga), which led to her becoming an apsara, then cursed to be a lotus for 100 years. The swan listens to her recitation of the chapter and is freed. The sage then explains the power of the tenth chapter.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 98)
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of the Vishvarupa chapter (Chapter 11 of the Bhagavad Gita) and narrates a story about a Brahmin named Sunanda who recites it, gains liberation, and later helps a village chief whose son was eaten by a rakshasa. The rakshasa reveals that recitation of the eleventh chapter can revive the dead and liberate him.

beats.title: The Farmer Cursed to a Demon and the Glory of the Eleventh Chapter
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a farmer who is cursed to become a demon for failing to save a bird from a vulture. A sage advises him to be freed by sprinkling water consecrated with the eleventh chapter of the Bhagavad Gita (Vishvarupa Darshana). Later, a Brahmin performs this, freeing the demon and the souls he devoured, who attain Vishnu's abode. The chapter also describes the glory of the eleventh chapter and includes a hymn to Mahalakshmi by a prince at Kolhapur.

beats.title: The Power of the Bhagavad Gita's Twelfth Chapter
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of a king who lost his sacrificial horse and sought help from the goddess Lakshmi, who directed him to a sage named Siddhasamadhi. The sage, through his spiritual power, retrieved the horse and later revived the king's father. The sage attributed his powers to daily recitation of the twelfth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita. The chapter also includes a sub-story about a sinful woman who was killed by a tiger, which was a reincarnation of a wicked Brahmin, and suffered in hell.

beats.title: Glories of Bhagavad Gita Chapters 13-16
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the glories of the 13th, 14th, 15th, and 16th chapters of the Bhagavad Gita through stories of liberation from hellish and animal births by hearing or reciting these chapters. It includes tales of a sinful woman who becomes a Chandala and later a dog, a king who hunts and witnesses miraculous events, and a horse that attains liberation upon hearing the 15th chapter.

beats.title: The Glories of the Fifteenth and Sixteenth Chapters of the Bhagavad Gita
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the glories of the fifteenth and sixteenth chapters of the Bhagavad Gita through stories of King Khangabahu, an elephant, and a servant named Duhshasana. It highlights the power of reciting and hearing the Gita's verses, leading to liberation from animal births and attainment of heaven.

beats.title: The Glory of the Eighteenth Chapter of the Gita and the Greatness of the Bhagavata Purana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of the eighteenth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, narrating how Indra, after losing his position, learns from Vishnu that reciting the eighteenth chapter of the Gita can restore his kingdom. Indra then finds a Brahmin who recites it daily, learns it, and attains liberation. The chapter also discusses the greatness of the Bhagavata Purana and includes a dialogue between Shaunaka and Suta about the best means to attain liberation in the Kali Yuga.

beats.title: The Lament of Bhakti in Kali Yuga
beats.summary: Narada describes the glory of the Bhagavata Purana, the story of King Parikshit's liberation, and his encounter with Bhakti (devotion) and her two sons Jnana (knowledge) and Vairagya (dispassion) in Kali Yuga. Bhakti laments her old age and her sons' decrepitude, and Narada explains the cause is the prevalence of adharma and lack of true devotion.

beats.title: Narada's Consolation to Bhakti
beats.summary: In this chapter, Bhakti (personified devotion) appears aged and distressed in Kali Yuga. Narada consoles her, explaining that Kali Yuga is actually favorable for devotion, and promises to establish her in every home. The chapter discusses the decline of other spiritual practices and the supremacy of bhakti.

beats.title: The Awakening of Bhakti through Srimad Bhagavata
beats.summary: Narada, after hearing Bhakti's distress, tries to awaken Jnana and Vairagya but fails. An akashavani advises him to perform a certain sadhana and consult saints. Narada searches but no one can help. He goes to Badrivan where he meets the Sanatkumaras, who explain that the Srimad Bhagavata recitation is the supreme means to revive Bhakti, Jnana, and Vairagya.

beats.title: The Glory of Srimad Bhagavata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the glory of the Srimad Bhagavata Purana, describing it as the essence of the Vedas and a means to attain liberation. It narrates how Narada instructed the sages Sanaka and others, and how they gathered at Haridwar to hear the Bhagavata Katha. The chapter emphasizes the supreme importance of listening to and reciting the Bhagavata, equating it with great sacrifices and holy places.

beats.title: The Glory of Srimad Bhagavata and the Appearance of Bhakti Devi
beats.summary: This chapter discusses the supreme glory of the Srimad Bhagavata Purana, especially its seven-day recitation (Saptaha Shravana). It describes how Lord Krishna, before leaving the earth, infused his entire essence into the Bhagavata. The chapter also narrates the appearance of Bhakti Devi (personified devotion) with her two sons in the assembly of sages led by Sanaka, and her conversation with them. The sages instruct her to reside in the hearts of devotees. The chapter emphasizes that in Kali Yuga, the Saptaha Shravana of the Bhagavata is the most effective means of liberation, surpassing yoga, meditation, and other practices.

beats.title: The Story of Atmadeva and the Fruit
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the power of the Saptahayajna (seven-day recitation of the Bhagavata Purana) to purify even the worst sinners in Kali Yuga. It then narrates the story of a Brahmin named Atmadeva who is childless and meets a sannyasi who gives him a fruit to give to his wife for a son. The wife, Dhundhuli, is quarrelsome and refuses to eat the fruit, lying to her husband.

beats.title: The Tale of Atmadeva, Dhundhukari, and Gokarna
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of a Brahmin couple, Atmadeva and Dhundhuti, who are childless. Dhundhuti's sister promises to give her own child to Dhundhuti after birth. The sister gives birth to a son, but later her own child dies, so she gives her son to Dhundhuti. The child, named Dhundhukari, grows up to be extremely wicked, while the Brahmin's wife later gives birth to a son named Gokarna, who is virtuous. Gokarna advises his father to renounce worldly attachments and practice devotion. Atmadeva follows the advice and attains liberation. After the parents' death, Dhundhukari continues his evil ways, associating with prostitutes and committing crimes.

beats.title: The Liberation of Dhundhukari
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Dhundhukari, a wicked Brahmin who was killed by his wives for his wealth. He became a fierce ghost, suffering greatly. His brother Gokarna performed rituals and sought help from sages and the Sun god, who advised reciting the Bhagavata Purana for seven days. During the recitation, Dhundhukari listened from inside a termite mound, which burst open each day. After seven days, he was freed from his ghostly form and attained a divine body, praising the power of the Bhagavata.

beats.title: The Power of Bhagavata Saptah
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana extols the power of listening to the Bhagavata Purana (Saptah) for seven days, which burns all sins and grants liberation. It narrates the story of Dhundhukari, a sinner who attains salvation by listening to the Katha, and later Gokarna organizes a Saptah where all listeners, including animals and outcastes, are taken to Vaikuntha by Lord Vishnu. The chapter also describes the proper method for conducting a Saptah.

beats.title: Instructions for Bhagavata Saptah
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana provides detailed instructions for organizing and conducting a seven-day recitation (saptah) of the Bhagavata Purana. It covers invitation of devotees, preparation of the venue, selection of a qualified speaker, daily rituals, rules for listeners, and the benefits of participation.

beats.title: The Saptah Recitation of Bhagavata Purana and the Divine Kirtan
beats.summary: This chapter describes the procedures and benefits of the Saptah (seven-day) recitation of the Bhagavata Purana, including the concluding rituals, the importance of Vishnu Sahasranama, and the story of Narada and Shukadeva. It also narrates the divine appearance of Lord Vishnu and the celestial kirtan, and provides the historical timeline of Bhagavata recitations by Shukadeva, Gokarna, and Sanakadi.

beats.title: Glory of Yamuna and Indradyumna Tirtha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of the Yamuna river and the Indradyumna tirtha. It recounts how King Shibi questioned sages Narada and Parvata about the sacrificial posts on the Yamuna bank, leading to the story of Indra's yajna at Khandava forest after Hiranyakashipu's defeat. Indra praises Lord Vishnu, Brahma, and Shiva, emphasizing Vishnu's supremacy and the power of devotion.

beats.title: The Establishment of Indraprastha and the Tale of Shivarama and Vishnusharma
beats.summary: Narada narrates to King Shibi the story of Indra's past and future, the establishment of Indraprastha, and the tale of the Brahmin Shivarama and his son Vishnusharma who remember their past lives and perform rituals at the Nigamodhodha tirtha.

beats.title: The Tale of King Dilipa and the Merchant Darabha
beats.summary: The sage Devala narrates to a merchant named Darabha the story of King Dilipa and his queen Sudakshina, who were childless. Devala explains that Darabha's wife's vow to Parvati was not fulfilled properly, causing her pregnancy to be fruitless. He advises them to worship Parvati together to obtain a son, citing the example of King Dilipa who served the cow Nandini and obtained a son.

beats.title: King Dilipa and the Cow Nandini
beats.summary: King Dilipa, son of Ikshvaku, is childless and seeks advice from sage Vasistha. Vasistha reveals that Dilipa once failed to pay respects to Kamadhenu's daughter, causing a curse. To obtain a son, Dilipa must serve Nandini, the granddaughter of Kamadhenu. Dilipa and his queen Sudakshina serve Nandini for 21 days. To test Dilipa's devotion, Nandini disappears into a cave where a lion (actually a manifestation of Shiva) seizes her. Dilipa prepares to fight the lion but is paralyzed by its gaze. The lion speaks, revealing its divine nature, and Dilipa offers his own life to save the cow. Impressed, the lion releases the cow and blesses Dilipa with a son.

beats.title: The Story of King Dilipa and the Divine Cow
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana narrates the story of King Dilipa and his devotion to the divine cow Nandini, which leads to the birth of his son Raghu. It also includes the tale of a Brahmin named Shivasharman who, after a curse from Durvasa, attains liberation at the Nigamoddharaka Tirtha. The chapter concludes with a discussion of various holy places and the importance of devotion.

beats.title: Glories of Tirthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of various tirthas (sacred places), especially Kosala, Madhuvana, Badarikashrama, Haridvara, and Pushkara. It narrates stories of sinners who attained liberation by having their bones immersed in these tirthas, including a barber named Chandaka who killed a Brahmin, a thief who died at Haridvara, and a Brahmin couple who went to Badarikashrama. The chapter emphasizes the power of tirthas to purify even the worst sins.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 121)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glories of various tirthas (holy places) in Indraprastha (modern Delhi), especially the Prayaga tirtha. It narrates stories of Pundarika and his brother Bharata, the courtesan Mohini, King Virvarma and his queen Hemangi, and a crow, snake, and tree that attained liberation. The chapter emphasizes the power of these tirthas to grant liberation and fulfill desires.

beats.title: The Glory of Indraprastha and Magha Snana
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Indraprastha and the importance of Magha bath. It narrates how King Yudhishthira, after hearing the greatness of Indraprastha from Sage Shaunaka, returned to Hastinapura and performed the Rajasuya sacrifice, inviting Lord Krishna from Dwaraka. The chapter also recounts the story of Bhima creating Bhima-kunda, the virtues of circumambulating the tirtha, and the dialogue between Vasishtha and Dilipa about the merits of Magha snana. Additionally, it includes the tale of Bhrigu and a Vidyadhara on Manikuta mountain, where Bhrigu explains the benefits of Magha bath.

beats.title: The Vidyadhara's Transformation through Magha Snana
beats.summary: A vidyadhara (celestial being) with a tiger-like face seeks advice from sage Bhrigu. Bhrigu explains that his ugliness is due to breaking a fast on Ekadashi in the month of Magha and then eating on Dwadashi. He advises the vidyadhara to perform Magha snana (bathing) and rituals to regain beauty. The story of King Pururavas is cited, who regained his beauty through Magha snana and penance. The vidyadhara follows the advice, becomes handsome, and lives happily. The chapter extols the virtues of Magha snana, stating it surpasses all other religious practices in all ages.

beats.title: The Penance of Vatsa and the Boon of Mṛgaśṛṅga
beats.summary: This chapter describes the ascetic practices of Vatsa, son of Kutsa Muni, who performs severe penance including bathing in the Kāverī river during Māgha month. Lord Viṣṇu appears, grants him the name Mṛgaśṛṅga, and promises to reside at the lake. Mṛgaśṛṅga returns home, serves his parents and guru, and emphasizes the importance of serving mother, father, and guru as the highest tapas.

beats.title: The Virtues of Magha-snana and the Tale of Mrigashringa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of a virtuous wife, the qualities of a suitable bride, the merits of Magha-snana (bathing in the month of Magha), and narrates the story of sage Mrigashringa who revives four drowned girls and liberates an elephant from its papiś body through the power of Magha-snana.

beats.title: The Story of Dharmagupta and the Elephant Rebirth
beats.summary: A merchant named Dharmagupta recounts his life story: he was a virtuous man who performed many pious deeds, but was deceived by a Kapalika who tricked him into alchemy, causing him to lose his wealth. Despite his misfortunes, he continued to bathe in the Ganges during Magha. After his death, due to the merit of Magha bathing, he was reborn as an elephant. Later, the sage Mrigashringa liberated the elephant and praised Yama, the god of death.

beats.title: The Glory of Yama and the Tale of the Revived Girls
beats.summary: This chapter describes the sage Mrigashringa's praise of Yama, the god of death, and his subsequent boon to revive a group of Brahmin girls who had died. The girls then recount their experiences in Yama's realm, detailing the different paths for the virtuous and the sinful.

beats.title: The Path to Yama's Abode and the Punishments of Sinners
beats.summary: This chapter describes the torturous path to Yama's abode and the punishments inflicted on sinners by Yama's messengers. It details various physical torments, the judgment by Yama and Chitragupta, and lists the names of the hellish realms.

beats.title: Description of Hells and the Glory of Bharatavarsha
beats.summary: This chapter describes various hells (narakas) and the punishments for sinners, emphasizing the importance of righteous deeds, especially in the holy land of Bharatavarsha. It also highlights the value of human birth and the need to worship Lord Narayana to avoid hellish torments.

beats.title: Pushkara's Journey to Yama's Realm
beats.summary: A Brahmin named Pushkara, a devotee of Vishnu, is mistakenly brought to Yama's realm. He sees the torments of sinners and learns about the causes of their suffering. The chapter emphasizes devotion to Vishnu, especially through Magha bathing, Ekadashi fasting, and chanting His names, as the means to avoid hell and attain liberation.

beats.title: The Liberation of Sinners by Pushkara and the Marriages of Mrigashringa
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pushkara, a devotee of Lord Purushottama, who visits the realm of Yama and witnesses the sufferings of sinners in hell. By chanting the names of Vishnu, he liberates them. Yama grants him a boon that those who remember his glory will be free from untimely death. The chapter also describes the marriage of sage Mrigashringa to four wives (Suvritta, Kamala, Vimala, and Surasa) and explains the eight types of marriage (Brahma, Daiva, Arsha, Prajapatya, Asura, Gandharva, Rakshasa, and Paishacha) and their consequences.

beats.title: Duties of a Householder
beats.summary: King Dilipa asks sage Vasistha about the characteristics of a faithful wife and the duties of a householder. Vasistha describes the daily routine of a householder, including purification, sandhya, worship, and hospitality.

beats.title: Conduct for Householders and Duties of a Faithful Wife
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the proper conduct for householders (grihasthas) and the duties of a faithful wife (pativrata). It covers rules for eating, offering food to ancestors and beings, daily routines, sleeping directions, and the ideal behavior of a devoted wife towards her husband.

beats.title: Duties of a Wife
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana provides detailed instructions on the duties and conduct of a wife, emphasizing devotion to her husband, household management, and personal behavior. It covers topics such as cleanliness, service to family, restrictions during menstruation and pregnancy, and the importance of treating the husband as a deity.

beats.title: The Life of Mrigashringa and Mrkandu's Pilgrimage to Kashi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the life of sage Mrigashringa and his family, including his sons Mrkandu and others, their marriages, and their devotion to dharma. It details Mrigashringa's eventual renunciation and attainment of Brahmaloka, and later rebirth as a son of Brahma. The chapter also narrates Mrkandu's journey to Kashi with his mothers, where he performs rituals and praises the glory of Kashi.

beats.title: The Birth and Determination of Markandeya
beats.summary: This chapter describes the installation of various Shiva lingas in Kashi, the story of Mrikandu's parents and their death, the birth of Markandeya, his upbringing, and his determination to worship Shiva to overcome his short lifespan of sixteen years.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 137)
beats.summary: Markandeya's encounter with Death (Kala) and his devotion to Shiva. Markandeya, having received parental permission, goes to the southern seashore, establishes a Shiva linga, and worships Shiva. Death arrives to take him, but Markandeya requests to finish reciting the Mrityunjaya Stotra. After a debate, Death swallows him, but Shiva emerges from the linga, strikes Death, and rescues Markandeya, who then praises Shiva.

beats.title: The Glory of Shiva and the Importance of Magha Bath
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Lord Shiva and the importance of Magha bath. It narrates how Markandeya attained immortality by praising Shiva, and then discusses various physical and mental tirthas (sacred places), emphasizing that true purification comes from controlling the senses and mind rather than mere external bathing.

beats.title: The Story of Sutrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of performing shraddha and tarpana at a tirtha without delay, the merits of bathing in a tirtha, and the specific observances for the month of Magha, including bathing, charity, and worship of Madhava. It also narrates the story of a Brahmin named Sutrata who accumulated wealth through improper means, lost it to thieves, and then repented, realizing the futility of greed.

beats.title: The Power of Magha Bath and the Lakshmi-Narayana Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana discusses the importance of various religious practices, especially the Magha bath, and the power of the Lakshmi-Narayana mantra for liberation. It includes a story of Suvrata who, despite neglecting many duties, attains heaven through a Magha bath, and a dialogue between King Dilipa and Vasistha, and later between Narada and sages, culminating in Lord Vishnu revealing the supreme mantra.

beats.title: The Glory of the Eight-Syllable Mantra and Devotion to Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the rules for giving the sacred mantra to a devotee, the glory of Lord Vishnu and the eight-syllable mantra 'Om Namo Narayanaya', the process of initiation, and various forms of devotion. It includes dialogues between Brahma and Vishnu, Vasishtha and King Dilipa, and Shiva and Parvati.

beats.title: Exposition of the Pranava and the Narayana Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter explains the meaning of the Pranava (Om) and the mantra 'Om Namo Narayanaya', discussing the relationship between Lakshmi and Vishnu, the nature of the jiva (individual soul) as a servant of the Lord, and the various names and attributes of Narayana (Vishnu) as the supreme being.

beats.title: The Cosmic Sacrifice and the Glories of Vishnu and Lakshmi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmic sacrifice of Purusha (Vishnu) from which the Vedas, animals, and the four varnas originated. It explains Vishnu's omnipresence, his role in creation and dissolution, and the nature of Lakshmi as his eternal consort and the supreme goddess. The chapter also includes a hymn to Lakshmi and emphasizes devotion to Narayana.

beats.title: Description of Vaikuntha and the Supreme Lord
beats.summary: This chapter describes the nature of the supreme reality, the distinction between the material and spiritual worlds, the structure of Vaikuntha, and the process of creation and dissolution. It explains that the Lord is beyond material qualities, the material world is perishable, and the spiritual world (Vaikuntha) is eternal, luminous, and the abode of Vishnu. The chapter also details the guardians of the gates of Vaikuntha, the divine city, and the throne of the Lord.

beats.title: Description of Vaikuntha and the Vyuhas
beats.summary: This chapter describes the divine form of Lord Vishnu in Vaikuntha, his consort Lakshmi, and the surrounding deities and attendants. It also details the various vyuhas (emanations) of Vishnu and their respective abodes, and concludes with a prayer by Mahamaya to the Lord for creation.

beats.title: Creation of the Universe and Description of Vaikuntha
beats.summary: This chapter describes the creation of the universe by Lord Vishnu. It details the emergence of Mahat-tattva, Ahankara, the five great elements, and the cosmic egg. Brahma is born from Vishnu's navel lotus, and Rudra appears from Vishnu's sweat. The four varnas are created from Vishnu's body. The chapter also describes the four vyuhas (Vasudeva, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Sankarshana) and the ten avatars of Vishnu. It concludes with a description of Vaikuntha and the importance of devotion to Vishnu.

beats.title: Matsya and Kurma Avatars of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the glory of Lord Vishnu's avatars, focusing on the Matsya (fish) and Kurma (tortoise) incarnations. It narrates the story of the demon Makara stealing the Vedas, leading to Lord Vishnu's Matsya avatar to retrieve them. Then it tells of Durvasa's curse causing Lakshmi's disappearance, prompting the churning of the ocean (Samudra Manthana) where Vishnu assumes the Kurma avatar to support Mount Mandara.

beats.title: The Churning of the Ocean and the Appearance of Lakshmi
beats.summary: This chapter describes the churning of the ocean by gods and demons, the emergence of various beings and objects including Lakshmi, the appearance of Vishnu, and the establishment of the Ekadashi vow.

beats.title: The Story of Hiranyakashipu and Prahlada
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of the demon Hiranyakashipu and his brother Hiranyaksha, who were originally the gatekeepers Jaya and Vijaya of Lord Vishnu, cursed by the sages Sanaka and others to be born as demons. Hiranyaksha was killed by Lord Vishnu in his Varaha (boar) avatar. Hiranyakashipu performed severe penance to obtain a boon of invincibility, then became arrogant and oppressed the gods. His son Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, refused to worship him, leading to conflict.

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 150)
beats.summary: The priest advises Prahlada to abandon Vishnu and worship Shiva, but Prahlada firmly declares Narayana as the supreme deity. Hiranyakashipu orders demons, serpents, elephants, fire, and poison to kill Prahlada, but all fail due to Vishnu's protection. Hiranyakashipu then challenges Prahlada to show Vishnu in a pillar; Narasimha emerges, terrifying the demons.

beats.title: The Narasimha Avatar and the Slaying of Hiranyakashipu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Narasimha avatar, where Lord Vishnu appears as half-man, half-lion to kill the demon Hiranyakashipu. It details the fierce battle, the destruction of the demon army, the killing of Hiranyakashipu by Narasimha, the appeasement of Narasimha's anger by Goddess Lakshmi, and the subsequent coronation of Prahlada as king of the demons.

beats.title: The Vamana Avatar and the Defeat of Bali
beats.summary: This chapter describes the Vamana avatar of Vishnu. It begins with the rule of the demon king Bali, son of Virochana, who conquered the three worlds. The sage Kashyapa, distressed by his son Indra's loss of kingdom, performs penance with his wife Aditi to please Vishnu. Vishnu appears and promises to help. Later, Aditi gives birth to Vamana, a dwarf brahmachari. Vamana goes to Bali's sacrifice and asks for three paces of land. Bali agrees despite Shukracharya's warning. Vamana then assumes a giant form, measures the earth in one step, the heavens in the second, and with the third step, pushes Bali to the netherworld. Bali, however, becomes a devotee and is blessed.

beats.title: The Descent of Ganga and the Avatars of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the three streams of the Ganga (Mandakini, Bhogavati, Vegavati), the descent of Ganga from Vishnu's feet to Shiva's head, and the stories of King Bhagiratha and sage Gautam bringing Ganga to earth. It then narrates the Vamana avatar (Vishnu as dwarf) and the Parashurama avatar, including the birth of Parashurama to Jamadagni and Renuka, his receiving a mantra from Kasyapa, the killing of Sahasrabahu Arjuna and other Kshatriyas, and his eventual retreat to the ashram of Nara-Narayana. The chapter concludes with a mention of Svayambhuva Manu's penance at Naimisharanya.

beats.title: The Boon to Manu and the Birth of Rama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the boon granted by Lord Vishnu to Svayambhuva Manu, the subsequent births of Manu as Dasharatha, Vasudeva, and a future Brahmin in Sambhal. It then narrates the story of Ravana and Kumbhakarna's birth from the sage Vishrava and Kaikesi, their boon from Brahma, and the oppression of the gods. Vishnu promises to incarnate as Rama in the Raghu dynasty to kill Ravana. The chapter details the lineage of Ikshvaku, King Dasharatha's marriage to Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi, the city of Ayodhya, and the divine birth of Rama from Kausalya after Vishnu gives Dasharatha a divine pudding.

beats.title: The Birth and Early Exploits of Lord Rama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth and early life of Lord Rama, including his naming ceremony by sage Vasistha, the divine signs on his body, the birth of his brothers Bharata, Lakshmana, and Shatrughna, the appearance of Sita from the earth, and the visit of sage Vishvamitra to Ayodhya to seek Rama's help in protecting his sacrifice from demons.

beats.title: Rama's Marriage and Early Adventures
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the story of Rama from his arrival in Mithila for the Vajapeya sacrifice, his meeting with Ahalya, marriage to Sita and other princesses, encounter with Parashurama, exile to the forest, and the crow incident with Jayanta.

beats.title: Rama's Exile and Adventures in the Forest
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Lord Rama's exile, including his encounters with the crow, sage Atri and Anasuya, the demon Viradha, sage Sharabhanga, and others. It describes the stay at Panchavati, the arrival of Shurpanakha, the killing of Khara and Dushana, the abduction of Sita by Ravana, the battle with Jatayu, and the meeting with Shabari.

beats.title: The Story of Rama
beats.summary: This chapter recounts the story of Rama from the Padma Purana, covering his journey to Pampa lake, killing the demon Kabandha, meeting Hanuman and Sugriva, building the bridge to Lanka, the war with Ravana, the death of Ravana and other demons, the coronation of Vibhishana, and Sita's trial by fire.

beats.title: Rama's Return and Coronation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the return of Lord Rama to Ayodhya after defeating Ravana, his reunion with Bharata and Shatrughna, the coronation ceremony with Sita, and the joyous celebrations involving sages, kings, vanaras, and rakshasas.

beats.title: Sita's Exile and Rama's Ascent
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the events following Sita's exile, her stay at Valmiki's ashram, the birth of her twin sons, Rama's Ashvamedha sacrifice, Sita's final test and return to the earth, Rama's conversation with Kala, the arrival of Durvasa, Lakshmana's departure to the divine realm, and Rama's instructions to his allies before his own ascent.

beats.title: The Departure of Rama and the Advent of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter describes the departure of Lord Rama and his followers to the Sarayu River, where they enter the water and attain liberation. It also begins the narrative of Lord Krishna's avatar, including the prophecy of his birth to Devaki and Vasudeva, and the earth's plea to Brahma and Vishnu for relief from demonic oppression.

beats.title: Birth and Early Pastimes of Krishna and Balarama
beats.summary: This chapter describes the birth of Lord Krishna and Balarama, their early life in Vrindavan, and the killing of the demoness Putana. It also includes the naming ceremony by Garga Muni and the episode of the overturned cart.

beats.title: Childhood Pastimes of Krishna in Vrindavan
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the childhood pastimes of Lord Krishna in Vrindavan, including the killing of various demons (Putana, Bakasura, Aghasura, etc.), the binding by mother Yashoda (giving the name Damodara), the uprooting of the Yamala-Arjuna trees, the stealing of calves by Brahma, the subduing of Kaliya, and the killing of demons such as Dhenukasura, Pralambasura, Arishtasura, and Keshi. It also mentions the move to Vrindavan and the eventual journey to Mathura.

beats.title: Krishna's Childhood Pastimes and Akrura's Visit
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the childhood pastimes of Lord Krishna and Balarama in Vrindavan, including the lifting of Govardhana Hill, the defeat of Indra's pride, and the subsequent visit of Indra. It also narrates the arrival of Akrura in Vrindavan, sent by Kamsa to invite Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the bow sacrifice, and Akrura's revelation to Nanda about Krishna's divine identity.

beats.title: अक्रूर का मथुरा गमन और श्रीकृष्ण का आगमन
beats.summary: अक्रूर व्रज से मथुरा के लिए बलराम और श्रीकृष्ण को ले जाते हैं। रास्ते में श्रीकृष्ण यमुना में स्नान करते हैं और अक्रूर उनका दिव्य रूप देखते हैं। मथुरा पहुँचकर श्रीकृष्ण एक रँगरेज को मारते हैं और एक माली को वरदान देते हैं।

beats.title: Complete, Chapter 1 (part 166)
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the arrival of Krishna and Balarama in Mathura, their encounter with the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who gives them sandalwood, the breaking of the bow, the slaying of the elephant Kuvalayapida, the wrestling match with Chanura and Mushtika, the killing of Kamsa and his brother Sunama, the reunion with Devaki and Vasudeva, the departure of Nanda and the gopas, and the release of Ugrasena.

beats.title: Post-Kamsa Events: Ugrasena's Coronation, Education, Jarāsandha's Attack, and Kālayavana's Death
beats.summary: This chapter describes events after Kamsa's death: Ugrasena is released and crowned king of Mathura, and the Yadava elders are given positions. Vasudeva performs the upanayana ceremony for Balarama and Krishna with Garga as acharya. They study under Sandipani and revive his dead son as guru-dakshina. Jarāsandha attacks Mathura with a vast army; Krishna and Balarama defeat him, but Krishna spares his life at Krishna's request. Jarāsandha then allies with Kālayavana, who besieges Mathura. Krishna builds Dvārakā and transports the citizens there. Krishna and Balarama fight Kālayavana; Krishna lures him into a cave where the sleeping king Muchukunda is, and Kālayavana kicks Muchukunda, who burns him to ashes with his glance.

beats.title: Liberation of Muchukunda and Marriage of Krishna
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Muchukunda's liberation by Lord Krishna, the defeat of Jarasandha, the gift of the Sudharma assembly hall to Krishna, the marriage of Balarama to Revati, and the detailed account of Rukmini's abduction and marriage to Krishna.

beats.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Syamantaka Gem
beats.summary: This chapter describes the marriage of Lord Krishna with Rukmini and other queens, the story of the Syamantaka gem, the killing of Narakasura, and the uprooting of the Parijata tree. It includes the marriage of Krishna with Rukmini, the story of Satyabhama and the Syamantaka gem, the fight with Jambavan, and the marriage with Jambavati. It also mentions the marriages with Kalindi, Mitravinda, Nagnajiti, and others, and the killing of Narakasura.

beats.title: Narakasura Vadha and Parijata Harana
beats.summary: This chapter describes Krishna's victory over the demon Narakasura, the recovery of stolen divine items, the liberation of 16,000 captive princesses, and the subsequent journey to heaven where Satyabhama is insulted by Indrani, leading Krishna to uproot the Parijata tree and bring it to Dwaraka after a brief conflict with Indra.

beats.title: The Abduction of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of Aniruddha's abduction by Usha, the daughter of the demon king Bana. Usha dreams of a handsome man and, with the help of her friend Chitralekha, identifies him as Aniruddha, the grandson of Krishna. Chitralekha magically transports Aniruddha to Usha's palace. They spend a month together until discovered. Bana sends soldiers to capture Aniruddha, but he defeats them. Bana himself fights and binds Aniruddha. Narada informs Krishna, who comes with Balarama and Pradyumna to rescue him. They encounter Shiva, who guards Bana's city due to a boon. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, and between their respective followers. Shiva releases a fever weapon, which Krishna neutralizes. The two fevers are sent to the mortal world. Bana then fights Krishna, who cuts off his arms. Shiva intervenes, reminding Krishna of Bana's boon of immortality. Krishna spares Bana, who releases Aniruddha and marries him to Usha.

beats.title: Krishna's Pastimes: Marriages, Battles, and Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter describes several episodes from Krishna's life: the marriage of Aniruddha and Usha, the killing of Paundraka and Dantavakra, the liberation of the Vraja residents, the story of Sudama, the killing of Jarasandha, the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death, the liberation of Nanda and Yashoda, and the story of the Brahmana who visited Krishna.

beats.title: The End of the Yadava Dynasty and Krishna's Departure
beats.summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of the Mahabharata war, including Krishna's return to Dwaraka, the story of a Brahmin whose son died and was revived by Krishna and Arjuna's visit to Vaikuntha, the curse of the Yadavas leading to their destruction, Krishna's death at the hands of a hunter, and the final departure of Krishna and the Yadavas to their divine abodes.

beats.title: The Glory of Vishnu and Methods of Worship
beats.summary: This chapter describes the glory of Lord Vishnu and Krishna, the methods of worship, and the importance of devotion. It narrates how Lord Krishna relieved the earth's burden, destroyed demons, and established devotees in the supreme abode. It also details the two types of divine images (established and self-manifested), lists sacred places where Vishnu is present, and prescribes daily rituals for devotees.

beats.title: The Procedure for Vishnu Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana describes the detailed procedure for the worship of Lord Vishnu, including the preparation of the sacred space, the use of mantras, the offering of various items, the performance of homa (fire sacrifice), and the meditation on Vishnu's form. It also warns against worshipping lower beings like yakshas, bhutas, and rakshasas, which leads to negative consequences.

beats.title: Worship of Vishnu and Vaishnava Conduct
beats.summary: This chapter describes the proper methods for worshipping Lord Vishnu and the conduct of Vaishnavas. It details the rituals for each month, the importance of avoiding sinful acts, and the superiority of worshipping Vishnu's devotees. The chapter concludes with Parvati Devi following the instructions of Vamadeva and reciting the Vishnu Sahasranama before meals.

beats.title: The Glory of Rama's Name
beats.summary: Parvati asks Shiva to eat before her, as she will eat only after reciting Vishnu Sahasranama. Shiva laughs and praises her devotion to Vishnu, but declares that the name 'Rama' is equal to a thousand names. He then recites a list of 108 names of Rama, explaining their meanings and glories.

beats.title: The 108 Names of Lord Rama and the Debate on Supreme Deity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Padma Purana presents the 108 names of Lord Rama, their meanings, and the glories of chanting them. It also includes a discussion among sages about the supremacy of deities, with the conclusion that Vishnu (as Rama) is supreme.

beats.title: Bhrigu's Test of the Trimurti
beats.summary: Sage Bhrigu, sent by other sages to determine the supreme deity, first visits Shiva but is denied entry by Nandi, leading him to curse Shiva's offerings. He then visits Brahma, who ignores him, prompting Bhrigu to curse Brahma to be unworthy of worship. Finally, Bhrigu goes to Vishnu, kicks him on the chest, but Vishnu responds with humility and devotion, praising Bhrigu's feet. Bhrigu then extols Vishnu's supremacy and his special relationship with Brahmins.

beats.title: Bhrigu's Test and the Supremacy of Vishnu
beats.summary: Bhrigu tests the Trimurti and declares Vishnu supreme; sages worship Vishnu; benefits of reciting Padma Purana.

beats.title: Untitled
beats.summary: The text appears to be a corrupted or garbled version of the Padma Purana, Chapter 1, with many unintelligible sequences. It likely contains traditional Puranic content such as cosmogony, genealogies, and teachings, but due to the corruption, no coherent narrative can be extracted.

beats.title: Untitled
beats.summary: The chapter appears to be garbled or corrupted, containing unintelligible text. No coherent narrative or teachings can be extracted.
